topics.title: Models of LTI Systems: Linear Models, State Space Models, OE Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT757 - SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION AND ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Models of LTI Systems: Linear Models, State Space Models, OE Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Linear Models of LTI Systems

A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system** is a system whose behavior is governed by linear, constant-coefficient difference (or differential) equations, where the system parameters do not change with time. In the discrete-time framework of digital control and identification, an LTI system is mathematically described by a linear difference equation relating past outputs, past inputs, and the current input to the current output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Canonical):** A linear model of an LTI system is any mathematical representation (transfer function, difference equation, or state-space) that obeys the principles of **superposition** (additivity) and **homogeneity** (scaling), and whose coefficients are time-invariant.

The general **linear difference equation model** of an LTI discrete-time SISO system is given by:

$$y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + a_2 y(k-2) + \cdots + a_{n_a} y(k-n_a) = b_1 u(k-1) + b_2 u(k-2) + \cdots + b_{n_b} u(k-n_b)$$

where $y(k)$ is the output at sampling instant $k$, $u(k)$ is the input, $a_i$ and $b_i$ are the constant model parameters, and $n_a$, $n_b$ are the polynomial orders (delays).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of an LTI system as a **black-box recipe**. If you double the ingredients (input), you double the cake (output) — that is *linearity*. If the recipe does not depend on the day of the week (time-invariant), it is *time-invariant*. The "model" is simply the mathematical recipe that predicts tomorrow's cake from today's cake and the ingredients.

### 1.2 State Space Models

A **State Space Model (SSM)** is a compact, time-domain representation of an LTI system that uses a set of **internal state variables** to fully capture the dynamic memory of the system. It is particularly powerful for **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output)** systems and is the natural form for modern control design (pole-placement, LQR, Kalman filtering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The state space model of a discrete-time LTI system is a coupled pair of equations — a *state equation* that propagates the internal state forward in time, and an *output equation* that maps the state (and input) to the measured output.

The standard discrete-time LTI state space model is:

$$x(k+1) = A \, x(k) + B \, u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C \, x(k) + D \, u(k)$$

where $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the state vector, $u(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the input, $y(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ is the output, and $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$, $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ are the **system matrices**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a **bath-tub with a leak**. The state variable $x(k)$ is the *amount of water currently in the tub*. The input $u(k)$ is the *new water being poured in*. The output $y(k)$ is the *water level reading on the gauge*. The matrix $A$ tells you *how fast the tub drains*, $B$ tells you *how fast the tap fills it*, and $C$ converts the *internal water volume into a visible reading*. Knowing the current state is enough to predict the next state — the past is summarized in the present.

### 1.3 Output Error (OE) Model

The **Output Error (OE) Model** is a specific parametric linear model used in system identification where the system dynamics (transfer function from $u$ to $y$) are modeled separately from the disturbance (noise) model. In the OE structure, the noise is assumed to enter the system **only at the output measurement**, after the deterministic dynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An OE model assumes that the measured output $y(k)$ equals the noise-free output of a linear dynamic system $G(z)$ driven by the input $u(k)$, plus an additive output disturbance $v(k)$ that does **not** affect the system's internal dynamics.

The OE model equations are:

$$x(k+1) = A \, x(k) + B \, u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C \, x(k) + D \, u(k) + v(k)$$

Equivalently, in polynomial form:

$$y(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} \, u(k) + v(k)$$

where $B(z) = b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}$ and $F(z) = 1 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + f_{n_f} z^{-n_f}$ are polynomials in the backward shift operator $z^{-1}$, and $v(k)$ is assumed to be **white noise** with zero mean and variance $\sigma_v^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of an OE model like a **weighing scale with a shaky hand**. The true weight of the object is determined *only* by the object's properties (the $B/F$ dynamics) — the shake (noise $v(k)$) is added *after* the scale has produced its reading. The dynamics themselves are clean; the noise cannot disturb the system's true behavior, only corrupt the measurement. This is the cleanest possible separation between "signal path" and "noise path."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Multiple Models Exist — The Identification Perspective

In **system identification**, we choose a model structure based on:
1. The assumed nature of the disturbance (white, colored, correlated with input).
2. The intended use of the model (prediction, simulation, control design).
3. The available prior knowledge about the plant dynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of model directly determines which **prediction-error method** is used, and whether estimates are consistent (asymptotically unbiased).

### 2.2 Detailed Structure of Each Linear Model

#### 2.2.1 Transfer Function (TF) Model
The most classical representation, valid for SISO LTI systems in the $z$-domain:

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_{n_a} z^{-n_a}}$$

#### 2.2.2 Family of Linear Polynomial Models (Equation-Error Family)
Let $A(z), B(z), C(z), D(z), F(z)$ denote polynomials in $z^{-1}$:

| Model Name | Equation | Noise Assumption | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FIR** (Finite Impulse Response) | $y(k) = B(z)\,u(k) + v(k)$ | White noise $v(k)$ | Open-loop, finite-memory systems |
| **ARX** (AutoRegressive with eXogenous input) | $A(z)\,y(k) = B(z)\,u(k) + v(k)$ | White noise $v(k)$ at output | Simple predictive models |
| **ARMAX** (AutoRegressive Moving Average with eXogenous input) | $A(z)\,y(k) = B(z)\,u(k) + C(z)\,v(k)$ | Colored noise via $C(z)$ | Disturbance modeling |
| **OE** (Output Error) | $y(k) = \dfrac{B(z)}{F(z)}\,u(k) + v(k)$ | White noise added after dynamics | Simulation, control-design models |
| **BJ** (Box-Jenkins) | $y(k) = \dfrac{B(z)}{F(z)}\,u(k) + \dfrac{C(z)}{D(z)}\,v(k)$ | Independent input & noise dynamics | Most general, robust identification |

#### 2.2.3 State Space Model
The state space form is the most general (MIMO-ready) and is the foundation of modern control theory:

$$x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k) + v(k)$$

Key structural properties determined by the matrices:
- **Stability:** The system is asymptotically stable if and only if all eigenvalues of $A$ lie strictly inside the unit circle of the complex plane ($\vert \lambda_i(A) \vert < 1$).
- **Controllability:** The pair $(A, B)$ is controllable if the controllability matrix $\mathcal{C} = [B \;\; AB \;\; A^2 B \;\; \cdots \;\; A^{n-1}B]$ has full rank $n$.
- **Observability:** The pair $(A, C)$ is observable if the observability matrix $\mathcal{O} = [C^T \;\; (CA)^T \;\; \cdots \;\; (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$ has full rank $n$.

#### 2.2.4 Output Error (OE) Model — In-Depth

The OE model is the "gold standard" for **open-loop simulation models** because it does not require the noise model to be identified — a great advantage when the disturbance physics are complex or unknown. The predictor used in OE identification is:

$$\hat{y}(k \vert \theta) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} \, u(k)$$

The prediction error is $e(k) = y(k) - \hat{y}(k \vert \theta)$, and the OE method minimizes the sum of squared prediction errors:

$$V_{OE}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{1}{2} \left[ y(k) - \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) \right]^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The OE model is **consistent** (asymptotically unbiased) only when the true noise is white. If the true disturbance is colored, OE parameter estimates will be biased — this is a critical KTU exam point.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Element** | **Formula / Description** | **Symbol / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| General SISO LTI Difference Equation | $A(z)\,y(k) = B(z)\,u(k)$ | $z^{-1}$ is the backward shift operator |
| Transfer Function | $G(z) = B(z) / A(z)$ | Ratio of polynomials in $z^{-1}$ |
| State Equation | $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$ | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ |
| Output Equation | $y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$ | $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ |
| Stability Criterion | $\vert \lambda_i(A) \vert < 1 \;\;\forall i$ | Eigenvalues inside unit disc |
| Controllability Matrix | $\mathcal{C} = [B \;\; AB \;\; \cdots \;\; A^{n-1}B]$ | Rank must equal $n$ |
| Observability Matrix | $\mathcal{O} = [C^T \;\; (CA)^T \;\; \cdots \;\; (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$ | Rank must equal $n$ |
| OE Model Equation | $y(k) = \dfrac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) + v(k)$ | $v(k) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_v^2)$ |
| OE Predictor | $\hat{y}(k) = \dfrac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k)$ | One-step-ahead estimate |
| OE Loss Function | $V_{OE}(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{1}{2} [y(k) - \hat{y}(k)]^2$ | Scalar cost to minimize |
| ARX Predictor | $\hat{y}(k) = B(z)\,u(k) - [A(z)-1]\,y(k)$ | Uses past outputs explicitly |
| Convolution Sum (FIR) | $y(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{n_b} b_i u(k-i) + v(k)$ | Truncated impulse response |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Model Type | Real-World Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| **State Space** | Autopilot design for aircraft, satellite attitude control, MIMO process control in chemical plants, Kalman filter design for GPS navigation. |
| **Transfer Function** | Classical Bode/Nyquist stability analysis, lead-lag compensator design, single-loop PID tuning in industrial controllers. |
| **OE Model** | Building thermal dynamics modeling, robotics arm identification, DC motor speed control simulation, automotive suspension modeling. |
| **ARX Model** | Adaptive cruise control, echo cancellation, weather forecasting where the noise is short-memory white. |
| **ARMAX Model** | Servo systems with load disturbances, economic time-series modeling. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function to State Space (Controllable Canonical Form)

**Goal:** Convert a SISO transfer function into state space matrices $(A, B, C, D)$.

**Given:** A strictly proper transfer function of order $n$:

$$G(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{U(z)} = \frac{b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_n z^{-n}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}}$$

**Step 1 — Cross-multiply to obtain the difference equation:**

$$(1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_n z^{-n}) Y(z) = (b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_n z^{-n}) U(z)$$

**Step 2 — Inverse-transform to the time domain:**

$$y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) + a_2 y(k-2) + \cdots + a_n y(k-n) = b_1 u(k-1) + b_2 u(k-2) + \cdots + b_n u(k-n)$$

**Step 3 — Define the state vector in controllable canonical form:**

$$x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \\ \vdots \\ x_n(k) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} y(k-n) \\ y(k-n+1) \\ \vdots \\ y(k-1) \end{bmatrix}$$

This is a natural choice — the state components are a chain of past outputs.

**Step 4 — Derive the state-update equation for each component:**

By definition $x_1(k+1) = y(k-n+1) = x_2(k)$ (simple shift). Similarly $x_2(k+1) = x_3(k)$, etc. For the last component, substitute from the difference equation:

$$x_n(k+1) = y(k) = -a_1 y(k-1) - a_2 y(k-2) - \cdots - a_n y(k-n) + b_1 u(k-1) + \cdots + b_n u(k-n)$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite using state vector components:**

$$x_n(k+1) = -a_n x_1(k) - a_{n-1} x_2(k) - \cdots - a_1 x_n(k) + b_n u(k-1) + \cdots + b_1 u(k-1)$$

**Step 6 — Assemble into matrix form:**

$$x(k+1) = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -a_n & -a_{n-1} & -a_{n-2} & \cdots & -a_1 \end{bmatrix}}_{A} x(k) + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{B} u(k)$$

**Step 7 — Derive the output equation.** From the difference equation, the current output is:

$$y(k) = -a_1 y(k-1) - a_2 y(k-2) - \cdots - a_n y(k-n) + b_1 u(k-1) + \cdots + b_n u(k-n)$$

Recognizing $y(k-i) = x_{n-i+1}(k)$ and shifting the input by one sample gives:

$$y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} b_n - a_n b_0 & b_{n-1} - a_{n-1} b_0 & \cdots & b_1 - a_1 b_0 \end{bmatrix} x(k) + b_0 u(k)$$

For a strictly proper system ($b_0 = 0$), the **controllable canonical form** reduces to:

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} b_n & b_{n-1} & \cdots & b_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most tested derivation** in KTU Module 1. Practice the case $n=2$ by hand for full confidence.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Transfer Function to State Space (Observable Canonical Form)

**Alternative structure** — choose states as delayed inputs instead of outputs:

$$x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} u(k-1) \\ u(k-2) \\ \vdots \\ u(k-n) \end{bmatrix}$$

By identical algebra, the **observable canonical form** yields:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 & \cdots & -a_n \\ 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ \vdots \\ b_n \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the observable canonical form is just the **transpose** of the controllable canonical form (with appropriate index reversal). This duality is a fundamental KTU conceptual question.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: OE Model to State Space Conversion

**Goal:** Express the OE model in state space form.

**Given:**
$$y(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) + v(k)$$

with $B(z) = b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}$ and $F(z) = 1 + f_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + f_{n_f} z^{-n_f}$.

**Step 1 — Define an intermediate noise-free signal $w(k)$ (the deterministic output):**

$$w(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k)$$

This means $w(k)$ satisfies:

$$w(k) = -f_1 w(k-1) - f_2 w(k-2) - \cdots - f_{n_f} w(k-n_f) + b_1 u(k-1) + \cdots + b_{n_b} u(k-n_b)$$

**Step 2 — Build the state vector from past values of $w$:**

$$x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_1(k) \\ x_2(k) \\ \vdots \\ x_{n_f}(k) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} w(k-n_f) \\ w(k-n_f+1) \\ \vdots \\ w(k-1) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Write the state propagation:**

$$x_i(k+1) = x_{i+1}(k), \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, n_f - 1$$

For $i = n_f$, use the difference equation:

$$x_{n_f}(k+1) = w(k) = -f_{n_f} x_1(k) - f_{n_f-1} x_2(k) - \cdots - f_1 x_{n_f}(k) + b_{n_b} u(k-n_b) + \cdots$$

Assuming $n_b = n_f$ for simplicity, the OE state space matrices become:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & & & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 1 \\ -f_{n_f} & -f_{n_f-1} & \cdots & \cdots & -f_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ b_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

**Step 4 — Add the output equation with additive noise:**

$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k) + v(k)$$

where $v(k)$ is the **measurement noise**, independent of the input and the state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The state $x(k)$ in the OE model represents the **true noise-free output** of the system, not the measured output. This is the key conceptual difference from ARX/ARMAX models where the state is influenced by the noise.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: OE One-Step-Ahead Predictor

**Step 1 — Start from the OE model and isolate the deterministic part:**

$$y(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) + v(k) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) = y(k) - v(k)$$

**Step 2 — The natural predictor is the deterministic output, since $E\{v(k)\} = 0$:**

$$\hat{y}(k \vert \theta) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k)$$

**Step 3 — The prediction error (innovation) is:**

$$e(k) = y(k) - \hat{y}(k \vert \theta) = v(k)$$

**Step 4 — This shows the OE model has a special property:** under correct parameter values, the residuals $e(k)$ are themselves white noise, allowing direct model validation via autocorrelation tests.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** in KTU — students must show that the OE predictor does *not* depend on past outputs, only on past inputs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Identification Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates the role of the model in a generic identification setup, showing how a chosen linear model class (Transfer Function, State Space, or OE) interacts with the input and noise to produce the measured output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode["Input u(k)"] --> plantNode["True Plant G_true(z)"]
    noiseNode["Disturbance v(k)"] --> outputNode["Measured Output y(k)"]
    plantNode --> outputNode
    inputNode --> modelNode["Candidate Model G_model(z, theta)"]
    modelNode --> predNode["Predicted Output y_hat(k)"]
    outputNode --> errNode["Prediction Error e(k)"]
    predNode --> errNode
    errNode --> optNode["Optimizer min V(theta)"]
    optNode -->|theta_hat| modelNode
```

### 4.2 State Space Model Architecture

This diagram shows the internal feedback structure of a state space realization. The state vector $x(k)$ is updated each sample using $A$ and $B$, and the output is formed using $C$ and $D$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uNode["u(k)"] --> bMulNode["Matrix B"]
    bMulNode --> sumNodeA["Sum x(k+1)"]
    aMulNode["Matrix A"] --> sumNodeA
    xPrevNode["x(k)"] --> aMulNode
    sumNodeA --> xNextNode["x(k+1)"]
    xNextNode --> xPrevNode
    xPrevNode --> cMulNode["Matrix C"]
    cMulNode --> sumNodeY["Sum y(k)"]
    uNode --> dMulNode["Matrix D"]
    dMulNode --> sumNodeY
    sumNodeY --> yNode["y(k)"]
    xPrevNode --> xNextNode
```

### 4.3 Output Error (OE) Model Block Diagram

The defining feature of the OE model: the noise $v(k)$ enters **only at the output**, after the dynamics block. Compare this with ARX/ARMAX where the noise would feed back into the dynamics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uNode["Input u(k)"] --> dynNode["Dynamics B(z)/F(z)"]
    dynNode --> sigOutNode["Noise-Free Output w(k)"]
    sigOutNode --> sumNodeY["Sum"]
    vNode["White Noise v(k)"] --> sumNodeY
    sumNodeY --> yMeasNode["Measured Output y(k)"]
    uNode --> predDynNode["Identified Dynamics B_hat/F_hat"]
    predDynNode --> predOutNode["Predicted y_hat(k)"]
    yMeasNode --> errSum["Error e(k)"]
    predOutNode --> errSum
```

### 4.4 Conversion Flow Between Model Forms

This diagram maps the relationships between the three principal model representations covered in this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tfNode["Transfer Function B(z)/A(z)"] --> ssf1["Controllable Canonical Form"]
    tfNode --> ssf2["Observable Canonical Form"]
    ssf1 --> ssf3["State Space (A,B,C,D)"]
    ssf2 --> ssf3
    ssf3 --> ssf4["ZOH Discretization for given h"]
    oeNode["OE Model B(z)/F(z) u + v"] --> ssf1
    oeNode --> polyNode["Polynomial Form"]
    ssf1 --> tfNode
    ssf3 --> tfNode
    oeNode -->|Add noise model| armaxNode["ARMAX B,C"]
    oeNode -->|Set F = A| arxNode["ARX B,A"]
```

### 4.5 Table — Model Selection by Application

| **Application Context** | **Best Linear Model Choice** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|
| Open-loop simulation of plant dynamics | **OE Model** | Clean separation of dynamics and noise. |
| Closed-loop predictive control (MPC) | **State Space** | Native form for prediction horizons. |
| Initial model from step-response data | **FIR or ARX** | Few parameters, no iterative optimization. |
| System with known colored disturbance | **ARMAX or Box-Jenkins** | Explicit disturbance model via $C(z)$ or $D(z)$. |
| Multi-input multi-output plant | **State Space (MIMO)** | Compact, scales naturally to many I/O. |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a state space model for a discrete-time LTI SISO system. Mention the significance of each matrix $(A, B, C, D)$ involved.*

**Model Answer:**
A discrete-time LTI SISO system can be represented in state space form as:

$$x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$$

- **$A$ ($n \times n$):** The **system matrix** — describes how the internal state evolves in time without external input. Its eigenvalues determine stability.
- **$B$ ($n \times 1$):** The **input matrix** — maps the scalar input $u(k)$ into the state-space.
- **$C$ ($1 \times n$):** The **output matrix** — maps the state to the measured output.
- **$D$ ($1 \times 1$):** The **feedthrough (direct) matrix** — represents the direct path from input to output (zero for strictly proper systems).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is an Output Error (OE) model? Why is the noise term in the OE structure assumed to be white?*

**Model Answer:**
An **Output Error (OE) model** is a parametric linear model in which the measured output is the sum of the deterministic output of a linear dynamic system $G(z) = B(z)/F(z)$ driven by the input $u(k)$ and an additive white noise $v(k)$:

$$y(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) + v(k)$$

The noise $v(k)$ is assumed to be **white** (zero mean, constant variance, uncorrelated across time) because:
1. The OE structure has **no noise-shaping filter**; any unmodeled correlation would leak into the parameter estimates and produce **bias**.
2. White noise is the simplest random process; it provides the cleanest identification target.
3. Under correct parameters, the OE prediction errors are themselves white, which is a verifiable model-validation test.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** *Obtain the controllable canonical state space representation of the discrete-time system whose transfer function is:*
$$G(z) = \frac{z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2}}{1 + 3 z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2}}$$
*Verify the stability of the resulting state matrix $A$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the polynomial coefficients:**
$A(z) = 1 + 3 z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2}$, so $a_1 = 3$, $a_2 = 2$.
$B(z) = z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2} = 0 + 1\cdot z^{-1} + 2\cdot z^{-2}$, so $b_0 = 0$, $b_1 = 1$, $b_2 = 2$.

Here $n = 2$, so the system is second-order.

**Step 2 — Form the controllable canonical $A$ matrix:**

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Form the $B$ matrix (always a column of zeros with a 1 at the bottom for controllable form):**

$$B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Form the $C$ matrix. For strictly proper systems, $C = [b_n \; b_{n-1} \; \cdots \; b_1]$:**

$$C = \begin{bmatrix} b_2 & b_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = b_0 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Verify stability via eigenvalues of $A$:**

$$\det(\lambda I - A) = \det \begin{bmatrix} \lambda & -1 \\ 2 & \lambda + 3 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda(\lambda + 3) + 2 = \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0$$

Solving: $\lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = (\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) = 0$, so $\lambda_1 = -1$, $\lambda_2 = -2$.

Since $\vert \lambda_1 \vert = 1$ lies **on the unit circle**, the system is **marginally stable** (not asymptotically stable). This is a critical observation: the system has an oscillation mode at the Nyquist frequency. To make it strictly stable, the denominator would need to be modified (e.g., $1 + 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}$).

**(b)** *For the system in part (a), write the OE model equations and derive the corresponding one-step-ahead predictor. Discuss what happens if the true noise is colored instead of white.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the OE model equations in polynomial form:**

The system dynamics from part (a) is $G(z) = B(z)/A(z) = (z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2})/(1 + 3 z^{-1} + 2 z^{-2})$.

The OE model becomes:

$$y(k) = \frac{b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2}} u(k) + v(k)$$

with $b_1 = 1$, $b_2 = 2$, $f_1 = 3$, $f_2 = 2$, and $v(k) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_v^2)$.

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply to get the implicit equation:**

$$(1 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2}) y(k) = (b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}) u(k) + (1 + f_1 z^{-1} + f_2 z^{-2}) v(k)$$

**Step 3 — Define the noise-free intermediate signal $w(k)$:**

$$w(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) = -f_1 w(k-1) - f_2 w(k-2) + b_1 u(k-1) + b_2 u(k-2)$$

**Step 4 — The OE one-step-ahead predictor is the deterministic output:**

$$\hat{y}(k \vert \theta) = w(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k)$$

Equivalently, the predictor can be written recursively as:

$$\hat{y}(k) = -3 \hat{y}(k-1) - 2 \hat{y}(k-2) + u(k-1) + 2 u(k-2)$$

**Step 5 — The prediction error is:**

$$e(k) = y(k) - \hat{y}(k) = v(k)$$

**Step 6 — Discussion of colored noise case:**

If the true noise $e(k)$ is **colored** (correlated across time), then the OE model is **misspecified** because it assumes white noise. The consequences are:
- **Bias in parameter estimates:** The least-squares minimization $V_{OE}(\theta) = \sum e^2(k)$ will find a $\hat{\theta}$ that minimizes the wrong criterion; the estimates converge to values that are *not* the true system parameters.
- **Correlated residuals:** After fitting, the residuals $e(k)$ will still show significant autocorrelation, indicating a poor model.
- **Remedy:** Use a more general model like **Box-Jenkins** or **ARMAX** that includes a separate noise model $H(z) = C(z)/D(z)$ to absorb the colored disturbance.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *Explain the controllable and observable canonical forms of a state space model. Derive both forms for the system:* **(7 marks)**

$$G(z) = \frac{2 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.06 z^{-2}}$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:** $a_1 = 0.5$, $a_2 = 0.06$, $b_1 = 2$, $b_2 = 1$, $b_0 = 0$, $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Controllable Canonical Form:**
The state vector is chosen as delayed outputs: $x_1(k) = y(k-2)$, $x_2(k) = y(k-1)$.

$$A_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -a_2 & -a_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.06 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_c = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C_c = \begin{bmatrix} b_2 & b_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_c = 0$$

**Step 3 — Observable Canonical Form:**
The state vector is chosen as delayed inputs: $x_1(k) = u(k-1)$, $x_2(k) = u(k-2)$.

$$A_o = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & -a_2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 & -0.06 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B_o = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C_o = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D_o = 0$$

**Step 4 — Verification of stability:** The characteristic polynomial of $A_c$ (and $A_o$ — they are similar) is:

$$\lambda^2 + 0.5 \lambda + 0.06 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{-0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 - 0.24}}{2} = \frac{-0.5 \pm 0.1}{2}$$

So $\lambda_1 = -0.2$, $\lambda_2 = -0.3$. Both lie inside the unit disc — the system is **asymptotically stable**.

**(b)** *Define the Output Error (OE) model. Compare the OE model with the ARX model in terms of (i) noise assumption, (ii) predictor structure, and (iii) suitability for control design.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The OE model is:

$$y(k) = \frac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) + v(k)$$

where $v(k)$ is white noise. The predictor $\hat{y}(k) = [B(z)/F(z)] u(k)$ depends **only on past inputs**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | **OE Model** | **ARX Model** |
|---|---|---|
| **Equation** | $y(k) = \dfrac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k) + v(k)$ | $A(z) y(k) = B(z) u(k) + v(k)$ |
| **(i) Noise Assumption** | White noise added at output only. Noise does not enter dynamics. | White noise at output, but the dynamics $A(z)$ also act on the noise path — implicitly shaping it. |
| **(ii) Predictor Structure** | $\hat{y}(k) = \dfrac{B(z)}{F(z)} u(k)$ — depends only on **past inputs**. | $\hat{y}(k) = B(z) u(k) - [A(z)-1] y(k)$ — depends on **past inputs and past outputs**. |
| **(iii) Control Design** | **Excellent** — provides a clean, unbiased dynamic model of the plant. Ideal for MPC, LQG, and adaptive control design. | **Risky** — past-output dependence causes the noise to leak into parameter estimates; ARX control design can be poor if disturbance is colored. |
| **Computation** | Nonlinear in parameters (because of $F(z)$ in denominator) — requires iterative optimization. | **Linear regression** — closed-form least-squares solution exists. |
| **Consistency** | Consistent **only if true noise is white**. | Consistent **only if true noise is white AND uncorrelated with input**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the OE model with the denominator as $A(z)$ instead of the separate $F(z)$ polynomial. Remember: the OE model **deliberately uses $F(z)$** to distinguish the dynamics polynomial from any AR-type noise filter.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In the controllable canonical form, students frequently invert the sign of the $a_i$ coefficients. Double-check: the **last row of $A$** is $[-a_n, -a_{n-1}, \ldots, -a_1]$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting that for **strictly proper** systems, $D = 0$ and $b_0 = 0$.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Claiming the OE model is consistent under any noise — it is **not**. State explicitly the white-noise condition.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **controllability** (about the state) with **observability** (about the measurements) and mixing up their respective matrices.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] An LTI system obeys superposition and time-invariance; the canonical discrete-time model is a **linear constant-coefficient difference equation**.
- [x] The **state** of a system at time $k$ is the minimum information required to predict all future behavior given the future inputs.
- [x] State space form: $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k)$, $y(k) = C x(k) + D u(k)$.
- [x] **Stability:** $\vert \lambda_i(A) \vert < 1$ for all $i$ (eigenvalues strictly inside unit disc).
- [x] **Controllability matrix:** $\mathcal{C} = [B \;\; AB \;\; A^2 B \;\; \cdots \;\; A^{n-1} B]$ — must have rank $n$.
- [x] **Observability matrix:** $\mathcal{O} = [C^T \;\; (CA)^T \;\; \cdots \;\; (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$ — must have rank $n$.
- [x] **Controllable canonical form:** last row of $A$ is $[-a_n, \ldots, -a_1]$; $B$ is a column of zeros with 1 at the bottom.
- [x] **Observable canonical form:** first row of $A$ is $[-a_1, \ldots, -a_n]$; $C$ is a row vector $[1, 0, \ldots, 0]$.
- [x] Controllable and observable canonical forms are **transposes** of each other (with index reversal).
- [x] The **OE model** is: $y(k) = [B(z)/F(z)] u(k) + v(k)$ with $v(k)$ white.
- [x] The **OE predictor** uses only past inputs, not past outputs.
- [x] OE is **consistent** only when the true noise is white; biased estimates arise under colored noise.
- [x] OE is the **preferred model for control design** because it provides a clean plant model decoupled from noise.
- [x] ARX is computationally simple (linear regression) but mixes dynamics and noise; ARMAX/BJ are more general.
- [x] State space models are **MIMO-ready** and the natural form for modern control (MPC, Kalman filter, LQG).
- [x] All four matrices $A, B, C, D$ together fully describe an LTI system in state space.
- [x] For a **strictly proper** system, $D = 0$ (no direct feedthrough from $u$ to $y$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Sets, Structures and Identifiability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT757 - SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION AND ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Model Sets, Structures and Identifiability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Model Set (Mathematical Definition)
A **Model Set** $\mathcal{M}$ is a collection of candidate models that the user believes could plausibly describe the observed dynamic system. Formally, it is a parameterized family of mappings from past inputs and outputs to predicted outputs:

$$\mathcal{M} = \{ G(\theta) \mid \theta \in \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d} \}$$

where $\theta$ is the **parameter vector** of dimension $d$, and $\Theta$ is the admissible parameter space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A model set is a *family* of models, not a single model. The identification procedure searches *within* this set to find the best member.

### 1.2 Model Structure
A **Model Structure** is a specific *rule* or *mathematical template* used to relate inputs $u(t)$, outputs $y(t)$, and noise $e(t)$. The structure fixes the *form* of the model (e.g., ARX, ARMAX, Output-Error) but leaves the *parameters* free.

### 1.3 Identifiability
A model structure is said to be **(structurally) identifiable** at a parameter $\theta_0$ if there exists a unique $\theta \in \Theta$ such that the model output $G(\theta)$ is indistinguishable from the true system $S$ on the given data, but distinguishable from all other $\theta' \neq \theta$.

---

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy: The Tailor's Atelier
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Tailor Designing a Custom Suit**
>
> - **Model Set** = The tailor's entire *catalogue* of suit designs (a library of possibilities).
> - **Model Structure** = One specific *cutting pattern* (e.g., "single-breasted, two-button"). The pattern is fixed, but the dimensions (chest, sleeve, waist) are adjustable.
> - **Parameters** $\theta$ = The customer's actual measurements (e.g., chest = 40 inches).
> - **Identifiability** = Whether the tailor, given enough measurements, can *uniquely* determine the suit size. If two customers have identical measurements but different body shapes, the model is **not identifiable** (more than one solution fits the data).
>
> Just as a good tailor needs distinguishable features to size correctly, a good model structure must allow unique parameter estimation from data.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Model Sets (KTU Taxonomy)

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Parametric** | Finite-dimensional $\theta$ | ARX, ARMAX |
| **Non-Parametric** | Infinite-dimensional (impulse response, kernel) | Step response, spectra |
| **Semi-Parametric** | Mix of both | Wiener/Hammerstein models |
| **Linear** | Linear in $u$ and $y$ | FIR, ARX |
| **Non-Linear** | Non-linear in signals | Neural Networks, NARX |
| **Black-Box** | No physical insight | ARMAX |
| **Grey-Box** | Partial physical knowledge | Physical parameters in ODE |
| **White-Box** | Fully derived from physics | First-principles |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Focus:** Module 1 concentrates on **linear, parametric, time-invariant (LTI)** black-box structures and their identifiability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Black-Box Model Structures

A general **linear time-invariant** discrete-time SISO system is conventionally written as:

$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = \frac{B(q^{-1})}{F(q^{-1})} u(t) + \frac{C(q^{-1})}{D(q^{-1})} e(t)$$

where the **delay operator** $q^{-1}$ satisfies $q^{-1} y(t) = y(t-1)$, and $e(t)$ is white noise. Different choices of which polynomials are present define different structures.

#### (i) FIR — Finite Impulse Response
$$y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + e(t), \quad B(q) = b_1 q^{-1} + b_2 q^{-2} + \cdots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}$$
- Pure **equation-error** structure, no feedback of past outputs.
- Parameters: $n_b$ coefficients $\{b_k\}$.

#### (ii) ARX — AutoRegressive with eXogenous input
$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + e(t)$$
- $A(q) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}$, $B$ as above.
- **Linear regression** in parameters $\Rightarrow$ analytically solvable.

#### (iii) ARMAX — ARX with Moving Average noise
$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + C(q^{-1}) e(t)$$
- $C(q) = 1 + c_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + c_{n_c} q^{-n_c}$.
- Noise has coloured (MA) structure.

#### (iv) ARARX / ARARMAX
$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + \frac{1}{D(q^{-1})} e(t)$$
- High-order AR noise model.

#### (v) Output-Error (OE) Model
$$y(t) = \frac{B(q^{-1})}{F(q^{-1})} u(t) + e(t)$$
- Noise enters **additively at output** $\Rightarrow$ noise-free output $\hat{y} = \frac{B}{F} u$.
- **No feedback of past $y$** $\Rightarrow$ no equation error.

#### (vi) Box-Jenkins (BJ) Model
$$y(t) = \frac{B(q^{-1})}{F(q^{-1})} u(t) + \frac{C(q^{-1})}{D(q^{-1})} e(t)$$
- Most flexible: separate description of dynamics ($B/F$) and noise ($C/D$).

#### (vii) State-Space (SS)
$$\begin{aligned} x(t+1) &= A x(t) + B u(t) + w(t) \\ y(t) &= C x(t) + D u(t) + v(t) \end{aligned}$$
- $\{w, v\}$ are process/measurement noise with covariance matrices.

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Heuristic (KTU high-yield):**
> - Use **FIR** when the system is known to be open-loop stable and noise-free.
> - Use **ARX** for quick, consistent, linear-regression estimates.
> - Use **ARMAX / BJ** when the disturbance spectrum is coloured (e.g., load disturbances).
> - Use **OE** when measurement noise dominates and the process is open-loop stable.
> - Use **BJ** as the *general-purpose* choice when you have no prior knowledge.

---

### 2.3 Identifiability — Conditions and Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Ljung, 1999):** The model structure $\mathcal{M}(\theta)$ is **globally identifiable** at $\theta_0$ if for all $N \to \infty$,
> $$\hat{\theta}_N \xrightarrow{\text{w.p.1}} \theta^*, \quad \text{with } \theta^* \text{ unique.}$$
> It is **locally identifiable** if uniqueness holds only in a neighbourhood of $\theta_0$.

**Practical sufficient conditions for identifiability:**

1. **Persistent Excitation (PE):** The input $u(t)$ must persistently excite the system of order at least $d$ (the number of parameters).
$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t) \varphi^{T}(t) > 0 \quad (\text{positive definite})$$

2. **No redundant parameters** in the structure (no over-parameterization).

3. **Closed-loop identifiability** requires either an external excitation signal, knowledge of the regulator, or a *causal* feedback structure (closed-loop identifiability theorem of Ljung–Söderström).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $\mathcal{M} = \{ G(\theta) : \theta \in \Theta \}$ | Definition of model set |
| 2 | $A y(t) = B u(t) + C e(t)$ | Generic linear SISO form |
| 3 | $A(q) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}$ | AR polynomial |
| 4 | $B(q) = b_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}$ | X polynomial |
| 5 | $C(q) = 1 + c_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + c_{n_c} q^{-n_c}$ | MA polynomial |
| 6 | $D(q) = 1 + d_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + d_{n_d} q^{-n_d}$ | Noise denominator |
| 7 | $F(q) = 1 + f_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + f_{n_f} q^{-n_f}$ | Dynamics denominator |
| 8 | $x(t+1) = Ax(t) + Bu(t) + w(t)$ | State-space dynamics |
| 9 | $y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t) + v(t)$ | State-space output |
| 10 | $\frac{1}{N} \sum \varphi \varphi^{T} \to R \; (R>0)$ | Persistent Excitation |
| 11 | $V_N(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \varepsilon^{2}(t, \theta)$ | Prediction Error loss |
| 12 | $\hat{\theta}_N = \arg\min_{\theta} V_N(\theta)$ | PEM estimator |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility
> [!NOTE]
> - **ARX** is the workhorse in **adaptive control** (self-tuning regulators) due to its linear-in-parameters nature, enabling recursive least-squares.
> - **BJ/OE** dominate **industrial process identification** (e.g., pulp-and-paper, refinery distillation columns) where measurement noise is significant.
> - **State-space** is the natural language for **MPC** and **Kalman-filter** design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: One-Step-Ahead Predictor for ARX

**Given structure:**
$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + e(t)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the polynomials:**
$$\left(1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}\right) y(t) = \left(b_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}\right) u(t) + e(t)$$

**Step 2 — Move all $y$-dependent terms except $y(t)$ to the right-hand side:**
$$y(t) = -a_1 y(t-1) - \cdots - a_{n_a} y(t-n_a) + b_1 u(t-1) + \cdots + b_{n_b} u(t-n_b) + e(t)$$

**Step 3 — Form the regression vector $\varphi(t)$:**
$$\varphi(t) = \left[-y(t-1),\, \ldots,\, -y(t-n_a),\, u(t-1),\, \ldots,\, u(t-n_b)\right]^{T}$$

**Step 4 — Form the parameter vector $\theta$:**
$$\theta = \left[a_1,\, a_2,\, \ldots,\, a_{n_a},\, b_1,\, b_2,\, \ldots,\, b_{n_b}\right]^{T}$$

**Step 5 — The predictor is obtained by setting $e(t)=0$ (conditional expectation):**
$$\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = \varphi^{T}(t) \theta$$

**Step 6 — The prediction error is therefore:**
$$\varepsilon(t, \theta) = y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t) \theta$$

This is **linear in $\theta$** $\Rightarrow$ admits the closed-form LS solution.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: One-Step-Ahead Predictor for ARMAX

**Given structure:**
$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + C(q^{-1}) e(t)$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange for $y(t)$:**
$$y(t) = \underbrace{-a_1 y(t-1) - \cdots - a_{n_a} y(t-n_a) + b_1 u(t-1) + \cdots + b_{n_b} u(t-n_b)}_{\text{regression part}} + e(t) + c_1 e(t-1) + \cdots + c_{n_c} e(t-n_c)$$

**Step 2 — Notice that $e(t)$ appears on the right.** Therefore the predictor is no longer a static linear regression.

**Step 3 — The *optimal* one-step-ahead predictor** (in mean-square sense) is the conditional expectation given past data $\mathcal{F}_{t-1} = \{y(k), u(k), k<t\}$:
$$\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = \mathbb{E}\left[y(t) \mid \mathcal{F}_{t-1}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Since $e(t)$ is white noise with $\mathbb{E}[e(t)]=0$ and $e(t)$ is independent of $\mathcal{F}_{t-1}$:**
$$\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = -a_1 y(t-1) - \cdots - a_{n_a} y(t-n_a) + b_1 u(t-1) + \cdots + b_{n_b} u(t-n_b) + c_1 \hat{\varepsilon}(t-1) + \cdots + c_{n_c} \hat{\varepsilon}(t-n_c)$$

**Step 5 — Where the prediction error is the residual:**
$$\hat{\varepsilon}(t) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t \mid t-1)$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite compactly** using $C(q^{-1}) e(t) = C(q^{-1}) [y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t)\theta]$:
$$\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = \frac{B(q^{-1})}{C(q^{-1})} u(t-n_k) + \left[1 - \frac{A(q^{-1})}{C(q^{-1})}\right] y(t)$$

This shows the ARMAX predictor involves the **filtered input** $\frac{B}{C} u$ — non-linear in parameters $\Rightarrow$ iterative (e.g., Gauss–Newton) solution required.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Persistent Excitation Condition

**Statement:** A quasi-stationary signal $u(t)$ is persistently exciting of order $n$ if the limiting covariance matrix
$$R_u(n) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \begin{bmatrix} u(t-1) \\ \vdots \\ u(t-n) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u(t-1) & \cdots & u(t-n) \end{bmatrix}$$
is **positive definite**.

**Step 1 —** Suppose $\theta$ is identifiable, i.e., the Hessian of $V_N(\theta)$ at the true $\theta_0$ is non-singular.

**Step 2 —** The gradient of the LS loss is:
$$\frac{\partial V_N}{\partial \theta} = -\frac{2}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t) \varepsilon(t, \theta)$$

**Step 3 —** Setting to zero at the optimum gives the *normal equations*:
$$\left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t) \varphi^{T}(t) \right] \hat{\theta}_N = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t) y(t)$$

**Step 4 —** The bracketed matrix tends to $R_\varphi$ as $N \to \infty$.

**Step 5 —** For $\hat{\theta}_N$ to converge to a *unique* $\theta_0$, we need $R_\varphi$ to be invertible $\Leftrightarrow$ $u$ is PE of order $d = n_a + n_b$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Identifiability of the ARX model requires the input $u$ to be **persistently exciting of order at least $n_a + n_b$**. A constant input is *not* PE; a PRBS (pseudo-random binary sequence) of appropriate length *is*.

---

### 3.4 Identifiability vs. Consistency vs. Bias — A Triad

| Property | Definition | Requires |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Structural Identifiability** | $\theta$ can be uniquely recovered from noise-free data | No redundant parameters |
| **Consistency** | $\hat{\theta}_N \to \theta_0$ as $N \to \infty$ | Correct structure + PE input |
| **Asymptotic Normality** | $\sqrt{N}(\hat{\theta}_N - \theta_0) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, P)$ | Above + finite variance |
| **Bias** | $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\theta}_N] \neq \theta_0$ | Mismodeled noise structure |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Model Set Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Data Z superscript N] --> B{Available Prior Knowledge}
    B -->|No prior| C[Black-Box Linear Structures]
    B -->|Partial physics| D[Grey-Box Structures]
    B -->|Full physics| E[White-Box First-Principles]

    C --> F{Noise Character}
    F -->|White noise| G[ARX Structure]
    F -->|Coloured noise| H[ARMAX / BJ Structure]
    F -->|Measurement noise only| I[OE Structure]

    G --> J[Model Set M parameterized by theta]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J

    J --> K{Input persistently exciting of order d?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Identifiable theta star unique]
    K -->|No| M[Non-Identifiable Bias or Divergence]

    L --> N[Prediction Error Minimization PEM]
    N --> O[Estimated Model M hat]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Linear Black-Box Family (SISO)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["Input u(t)"] -->|q superscript nk delay| B["B of q inverse Filter"]
    B --> DYN["Dynamics B/F or B/A"]
    DYN --> OUT["Noise-free output w(t)"]
    NOISE["White Noise e(t)"] --> NOISEF["Noise Filter C/D or C/A or 1"]
    NOISEF --> NOISEOUT["Coloured Noise v(t)"]
    OUT --> SUM((+))
    NOISEOUT --> SUM
    SUM --> Y["Measured Output y(t)"]

    YFB["y(t) feedback"] --> A["A of q inverse Polynomial"]
    A -->|equation error| SUM
```

### 4.3 The Identification Loop (Ljung's Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SYS["True System S"] -->|generates| DATA["Data Z superscript N = y u"]
    DATA --> MODEL["Candidate Model M of theta from M"]
    MODEL --> PRED["Predictor hat y t given t minus 1"]
    PRED --> ERR["Prediction Error epsilon t theta"]
    DATA --> ERR
    ERR --> LOSS["Loss V_N of theta = 1/N sum epsilon squared"]
    LOSS --> OPT["Minimizer hat theta"]
    OPT --> VALID["Cross-Validation"]
    VALID -->|valid| FINAL["Final Model M hat"]
    VALID -->|invalid| BACK[Return to MODEL]
    BACK --> MODEL
```

### 4.4 Mapping of Model Structures (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["General Linear SISO: A y = B/F u + C/D e"]
    ROOT --> R1["F=1, C=1, D=1: ARX"]
    ROOT --> R2["C included: ARMAX"]
    ROOT --> R3["A=1, D=1, F=1: FIR"]
    ROOT --> R4["A=1, C=1: OE"]
    ROOT --> R5["A=1: Box-Jenkins BJ"]
    R1 --> R11["Linear regression -- LS solvable"]
    R2 --> R22["Nonlinear in theta -- iterative"]
    R4 --> R44["Output-error -- Gauss Newton"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define a model set and a model structure. How are they related?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **model set** $\mathcal{M}$ is a family of candidate models $\{G(\theta) : \theta \in \Theta\}$ parameterized by a finite-dimensional vector $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$. (1 mark)
- A **model structure** is the *specific functional form* (e.g., ARX, ARMAX, state-space) chosen to relate inputs, outputs, and noise. It is the *template* that generates the model set once the parameters are varied. (1 mark)
- **Relationship:** A model set is *generated* by a model structure. The structure fixes the form, the set contains all instances obtained by varying $\theta$. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*What is meant by identifiability of a model structure? State one sufficient condition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Identifiability** is the property that the parameter $\theta$ of the model structure can be uniquely determined from observations of input–output data. (1 mark)
- A structure is **globally identifiable** at $\theta_0$ if for all $N \to \infty$ and noise-free data, no other $\theta' \neq \theta_0$ produces the same input–output mapping. (1 mark)
- **Sufficient condition:** The input $u(t)$ must be **persistently exciting (PE)** of order at least $d = n_a + n_b$, i.e., the matrix $\frac{1}{N} \sum \varphi(t) \varphi^{T}(t) \to R$ with $R > 0$. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU ESE Pattern]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the one-step-ahead predictor for the **ARX** structure
$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + e(t).$$
Show that the resulting model is *linear in the parameters* and hence directly amenable to the **least-squares** method. Define the regression vector $\varphi(t)$ and parameter vector $\theta$ explicitly.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare the **ARX**, **ARMAX**, and **Output-Error (OE)** model structures in terms of (i) predictor form, (ii) linearity in parameters, (iii) handling of coloured noise, and (iv) typical industrial use-case. Tabulate your answer.

---

**Solution to (a):**

Starting from
$$A(q^{-1}) y(t) = B(q^{-1}) u(t) + e(t)$$

expand the polynomials:
$$\left(1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}\right) y(t) = \left(b_1 q^{-1} + \cdots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}\right) u(t) + e(t)$$

Rearranging:
$$y(t) = -a_1 y(t-1) - a_2 y(t-2) - \cdots - a_{n_a} y(t-n_a) + b_1 u(t-1) + \cdots + b_{n_b} u(t-n_b) + e(t)$$

The optimal one-step-ahead predictor (setting $e(t) = 0$ as $\mathbb{E}[e(t)\vert\mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = 0$):
$$\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = \varphi^{T}(t)\, \theta$$

where
$$\varphi(t) = \big[-y(t-1),\, -y(t-2),\, \ldots,\, -y(t-n_a),\, u(t-1),\, \ldots,\, u(t-n_b)\big]^{T}$$

$$\theta = \big[a_1,\, a_2,\, \ldots,\, a_{n_a},\, b_1,\, b_2,\, \ldots,\, b_{n_b}\big]^{T}$$

Since $\varepsilon(t, \theta) = y(t) - \varphi^{T}(t) \theta$ is *linear in $\theta$*, the LS estimator
$$\hat{\theta}_N^{LS} = \left[\sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t) \varphi^{T}(t)\right]^{-1} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \varphi(t) y(t)$$
exists in closed form, provided the bracketed matrix is invertible (PE condition).

---

**Solution to (b):**

| Property | ARX | ARMAX | OE |
|----------|-----|-------|-----|
| Equation | $A y = B u + e$ | $A y = B u + C e$ | $y = (B/F) u + e$ |
| Predictor | $\hat{y} = \varphi^{T} \theta$ | $\hat{y}$ involves $\hat{\varepsilon}$ history | $\hat{y} = (B/F) u$ (no $y$ feedback) |
| Linearity in $\theta$ | **Yes — linear regression** | **No — iterative required** | **No — iterative required** |
| Handles coloured noise | Poorly (limited) | **Yes** (MA polynomial) | Limited (assumes white output error) |
| Industrial use | Quick estimates, adaptive control | Disturbance-dominated loops | Open-loop stable processes with sensor noise |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define **identifiability** of a model structure. State and explain the **persistent excitation** condition. Why is a constant (DC) input *not* persistently exciting?

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For the **state-space** model
$$x(t+1) = A x(t) + B u(t) + w(t), \quad y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t) + v(t),$$
explain why this is *not uniquely identifiable* without additional constraints. List **two standard canonical forms** used to resolve this non-uniqueness, and write the associated parameter vector for each.

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Definition:** A model structure $\mathcal{M}$ is **identifiable** at $\theta_0$ if the model output is *uniquely* parameterized by $\theta_0$ — i.e., no other $\theta' \neq \theta_0$ generates the same input–output behaviour for all admissible inputs.

**Persistent Excitation (PE):** A signal $u(t)$ is PE of order $n$ if
$$R_u(n) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \begin{bmatrix} u(t-1) \\ \vdots \\ u(t-n) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u(t-1) & \cdots & u(t-n) \end{bmatrix}$$
is **positive definite** ($R_u(n) > 0$).

**Why DC fails:** A constant $u(t) = c$ gives $\varphi(t) = [-y(t-1),\ldots,-y(t-n_a), c, c, \ldots, c]^{T}$, and the resulting covariance matrix has rank $\le n_a + 1 < n_a + n_b$ when $n_b > 1$. Hence $R_u$ is **singular**, the normal equations are singular, and $\theta$ cannot be uniquely recovered.

**Implication:** The input spectrum must be *rich* — typically a PRBS (Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence), filtered white noise, or a multi-sinusoidal signal with at least $d$ distinct frequency components, where $d$ is the parameter dimension.

---

**Solution to (b):**

A generic state-space model has redundancy in the state coordinates: any non-singular transformation $T$ gives an equivalent realization
$$\tilde{A} = T A T^{-1}, \quad \tilde{B} = T B, \quad \tilde{C} = C T^{-1}$$
with **identical** input–output behaviour. Hence the parameters $(A, B, C, D)$ are **not uniquely identifiable** without fixing a basis.

**Two canonical forms:**

1. **Observable Canonical Form (OCF):** Constrains $C = [1,\, 0,\, \ldots,\, 0]$ and the companion structure of $A$, $B$.
2. **Controller / Reachable Canonical Form (CCF):** Constrains $B = [0, \ldots, 0, 1]^{T}$ and a companion structure of $A, C$.
3. *(Bonus)* **Modal Form:** $A$ is diagonal (real eigenvalues) or block-diagonal (complex conjugate pairs).

**Parameter vector example (OCF, $n=2$):**
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 & 1 \\ -a_2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$
$$\theta = [a_1, a_2, b_1, b_2]^{T} \quad (\text{4 unique parameters for SISO 2nd order})$$

This canonical-form parameterization makes the model **identifiable** by enforcing a unique representation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "order" in PE:** Students often say "input must be PE" without specifying *of order $d$*. This loses 1–2 marks. Always state the order explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing model *set* with model *structure*:** Marks are deducted for using these interchangeably. Memorize: *set = collection*; *structure = template*.
> 3. **OE vs. ARMAX noise handling:** Many students wrongly claim OE handles coloured noise. It does **not** — it assumes white measurement noise only.
> 4. **State-space identifiability:** A common error is to claim the SS model is *fully* identifiable. It is **not**, without a canonical form.
> 5. **Skipping the regression vector definition in ARX derivations:** Examiners specifically test $\varphi(t)$ and $\theta$ — drawing them earns full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Model Set** = $\{G(\theta) : \theta \in \Theta\}$ — a *family* of candidate models.
> ✅ **Model Structure** = the *functional template* (e.g., ARX, ARMAX, OE, BJ, SS).
> ✅ **ARX** is the only structure with a predictor **linear in $\theta$** $\Rightarrow$ closed-form LS.
> ✅ **ARMAX** has coloured noise via the $C(q^{-1})$ polynomial $\Rightarrow$ iterative PEM.
> ✅ **OE** = no $y$ feedback; only the input is filtered $\Rightarrow$ suitable for sensor noise.
> ✅ **BJ** = most general linear structure; separate dynamics and noise polynomials.
> ✅ **State-space** = infinite canonical equivalence classes; **not identifiable** without a canonical form (OCF, CCF, modal).
> ✅ **Persistent Excitation (PE)** of order $d$ = necessary for parameter identifiability.
> ✅ **PRBS** of sufficient length is the standard PE input in practice.
> ✅ **Identifiability ≠ Consistency:** Structure must be identifiable AND input must be PE AND data length must be sufficient.
> ✅ **Closed-loop identifiability** requires either external excitation, known regulator, or an open-loop-like input design (Ljung–Söderström theorem).
> ✅ **Three KTU-favourite structures to draw:** ARX block diagram, OE block diagram, and state-space realization.
> ✅ **Key equation for exams:**
> $$\hat{y}(t \mid t-1) = \varphi^{T}(t) \theta \quad \text{(ARX)}$$
> $$\hat{\theta}_N^{LS} = \left(\sum \varphi \varphi^{T}\right)^{-1} \sum \varphi y \quad \text{(LS estimate)}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models for Time-Varying and Nonlinear Systems: Models with Nonlinearities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT757 - SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION AND ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Models for Time-Varying and Nonlinear Systems: Models with Nonlinearities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Time-Varying vs. Time-Invariant Systems

A **time-varying system** is one whose parameters or dynamic characteristics change explicitly with time. The governing differential (or difference) equation contains coefficients that are functions of the absolute time variable $t$ (or discrete index $k$), not just the input/output signals.

A **time-invariant system**, by contrast, has parameters that remain constant regardless of when the system is excited; a shift in the input simply produces an identical shift in the output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A system is *time-varying* if its description includes an explicit dependence on the independent variable (time $t$ or sample $k$). A system is *nonlinear* if it does not satisfy the superposition principle: the response to $\alpha u_1(t) + \beta u_2(t)$ is not equal to $\alpha y_1(t) + \beta y_2(t)$ for arbitrary scalars $\alpha, \beta$.

## 1.2 What Are Nonlinearities in System Models?

A **nonlinearity** is any departure from the linear superposition law. In practical engineering plants (chemical reactors, servo motors, aerospace vehicles, biomedical processes), the static and dynamic relationships between inputs and outputs are rarely perfectly linear. Common sources of nonlinearity include:

- **Static nonlinearities (memoryless):** saturation, dead-zone, backlash, relay/hysteresis, square-law, square-root.
- **Dynamic nonlinearities (with memory):** multiplicative interaction of signals (bilinear terms), polynomial state dependence, Volterra kernels, fading memory in Wiener/Hammerstein blocks.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Audiometer with a Clutch"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine driving a car. A **linear system** is like driving on a perfectly flat, smooth highway at constant engine temperature — pressing the accelerator twice as hard always produces exactly twice the speed change. A **time-varying system** is like driving that same car while the engine slowly warms up: pressing the accelerator by 10% at start gives less response than pressing it 10% after 20 minutes, even though the road is identical. A **nonlinear system** is like driving a car that is sluggish below 20 km/h, perfectly responsive between 40–80 km/h, and saturates above 120 km/h. The same accelerator input produces dramatically different behaviour depending on the operating region. **Block-oriented models** (Hammerstein, Wiener) are like breaking the car into two physical stages: a *nonlinear pedal* followed by a *linear engine* (Hammerstein) or a *linear engine* followed by a *nonlinear gearbox* (Wiener).

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Specialized Models?

Standard linear ARX/ARMAX/Box-Jenkins structures used in linear identification **fail** when applied to nonlinear plants because:
1. They cannot capture amplitude-dependent gain (saturation, dead-zone).
2. They produce biased parameter estimates when fitted to nonlinear data.
3. They under-predict outputs at high amplitudes and over-predict at low amplitudes.

Hence KTU 2024 Scheme specifically mandates the study of dedicated **nonlinear black-box structures**.

## 1.5 Taxonomy of Nonlinear Models Covered in This Topic

| Model Family | Key Feature | Memory Source | Static Nonlinearity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hammerstein | NL → L cascade | Linear block | Input-side |
| Wiener | L → NL cascade | Linear block | Output-side |
| Hammerstein-Wiener | NL → L → NL | Linear block | Both sides |
| Volterra Series | General polynomial kernel | Distributed | Implicit |
| Bilinear | $u(k) \cdot x(k)$ cross-terms | State-coupled | Multiplicative |
| NARX / NARMAX | Polynomial in past I/O | Time-lagged | Polynomial |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hammerstein Model (Nonlinearity at Input)

### 2.1.1 Block Structure
The Hammerstein model cascades a **static (memoryless) nonlinearity** followed by a **linear dynamic system**.

$$
u(k) \;\xrightarrow{\;f(\cdot)\;}\; v(k) \;\xrightarrow{\;G(q)\;}\; y(k)
$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is a static nonlinearity and $G(q)$ is a linear transfer-function operator.

### 2.1.2 Mathematical Description

Let the static nonlinearity be represented by a finite-order polynomial of degree $m$:

$$
v(k) = f(u(k)) = \gamma_1 u(k) + \gamma_2 u^2(k) + \gamma_3 u^3(k) + \dots + \gamma_m u^m(k)
$$

The linear dynamic block is described by a stable, causal LTI transfer function:

$$
G(q) = \frac{B(q)}{A(q)} = \frac{b_1 q^{-1} + b_2 q^{-2} + \dots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}}{1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \dots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}}
$$

Hence the measurable output is:

$$
y(k) = G(q)\,\bigl[\,\gamma_1 u(k) + \gamma_2 u^2(k) + \dots + \gamma_m u^m(k)\,\bigr] + e(k)
$$

where $e(k)$ is a zero-mean white noise disturbance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Identification Trick (Key/Special Variables Method):**
> Because $G(q)$ is linear, the model is *linear in parameters* once the *nonlinearity coefficients* $\gamma_i$ are known (or vice versa). KTU examiners often award marks for recognizing this *bilinear-in-parameters* structure that allows iterative least-squares identification.

---

## 2.2 The Wiener Model (Nonlinearity at Output)

### 2.2.1 Block Structure
The Wiener model cascades a **linear dynamic block** followed by a **static nonlinearity**.

$$
u(k) \;\xrightarrow{\;G(q)\;}\; w(k) \;\xrightarrow{\;h(\cdot)\;}\; y(k)
$$

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Description

The linear block produces an intermediate signal:

$$
w(k) = G(q)\,u(k) = \frac{B(q)}{A(q)}\,u(k)
$$

The output static nonlinearity is again expressed as a polynomial:

$$
y(k) = h(w(k)) = \beta_1 w(k) + \beta_2 w^2(k) + \dots + \beta_p w^p(k) + e(k)
$$

Substituting:

$$
y(k) = \beta_1\,G(q)u(k) + \beta_2\,\bigl[G(q)u(k)\bigr]^2 + \dots + \beta_p\,\bigl[G(q)u(k)\bigr]^p + e(k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction from Hammerstein:**
> In the Hammerstein model, the polynomial operates on the **raw input $u(k)$** before filtering. In the Wiener model, the polynomial operates on the **filtered intermediate $w(k)$**, which makes the Wiener model **nonlinear-in-parameters** and significantly harder to identify using plain least squares.

---

## 2.3 The Hammerstein-Wiener (Sandwich) Model

The most general single-loop block-oriented nonlinear model sandwiches a linear dynamic block between two static nonlinearities:

$$
u(k) \;\xrightarrow{\;f(\cdot)\;}\; v(k) \;\xrightarrow{\;G(q)\;}\; w(k) \;\xrightarrow{\;h(\cdot)\;}\; y(k)
$$

Mathematically:

$$
v(k) = f(u(k)) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\gamma_i\,u^i(k)
$$

$$
w(k) = G(q)\,v(k) = \frac{B(q)}{A(q)}\,v(k)
$$

$$
y(k) = h(w(k)) + e(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{p}\beta_j\,w^j(k) + e(k)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Justification:** Many industrial actuators exhibit input saturation (input NL) while sensors exhibit output compression (output NL) — the Hammerstein-Wiener structure captures both effects simultaneously.

---

## 2.4 Volterra Series Model

### 2.4.1 Concept
The Volterra series is the **nonlinear generalization of the convolution integral**. It expresses the output as a sum of multi-dimensional convolutions of the input with Volterra kernels of increasing order.

### 2.4.2 Continuous-Time Form

$$
y(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{\infty}\!\dots\!\int_{0}^{\infty} h_n(\tau_1,\dots,\tau_n)\,\prod_{i=1}^{n} u(t-\tau_i)\,d\tau_1\dots d\tau_n
$$

### 2.4.3 Discrete-Time Form (Truncated, Order $N$)

$$
y(k) = \sum_{n=1}^{N}\sum_{\tau_1=0}^{M-1}\dots\sum_{\tau_n=0}^{M-1} h_n(\tau_1,\dots,\tau_n)\,\prod_{i=1}^{n} u(k-\tau_i)
$$

The kernels $h_1, h_2, h_3, \dots$ reduce to the standard impulse response ($n=1$), second-order kernel ($n=2$, captures inter-modulation/harmonic distortion), third-order kernel ($n=3$), etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** The number of parameters grows **exponentially** with memory length $M$ and order $N$. For $M=10, N=3$, the third-order kernel alone contains $10^3 = 1000$ parameters. Hence **truncated and structured** Volterra (Laguerre, Kautz, or block-oriented variants) are preferred in practice.

---

## 2.5 Bilinear Model

The bilinear model introduces multiplicative cross-terms between the input and the state/output:

$$
y(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_a}a_i\,y(k-i) + \sum_{j=1}^{n_b}b_j\,u(k-j) + \sum_{m=1}^{n_c}\sum_{l=1}^{n_d} c_{m,l}\,u(k-m)\,y(k-l) + e(k)
$$

The double-summation term is the **bilinear cross-product block** that captures phenomena such as amplitude-dependent damping and is intermediate between linear and fully nonlinear.

---

## 2.6 NARX and NARMAX Models

The **Nonlinear AutoRegressive with eXogenous input (NARX)** model expresses the current output as a nonlinear function of past outputs and past inputs:

$$
y(k) = F^{\text{NL}}\bigl[y(k-1), y(k-2), \dots, y(k-n_y),\, u(k-1), \dots, u(k-n_u)\bigr] + e(k)
$$

The standard choice (often used in KTU exam questions) is a **polynomial NARX**:

$$
y(k) = \sum_{i,j} c_{i,j}\,u^i(k-d)\,y^j(k-d) + e(k)
$$

When the past noise terms $e(k-1), \dots, e(k-n_e)$ are also regressed, we obtain the **NARMAX** model.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Model | Core Equation | Parameter Count | Linearity in Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hammerstein | $y(k) = G(q) \sum_{i=1}^{m} \gamma_i u^i(k) + e(k)$ | $n_a + n_b + m - 1$ | Bilinear |
| 2 | Wiener | $y(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j [G(q)u(k)]^j + e(k)$ | $n_a + n_b + p - 1$ | Nonlinear |
| 3 | Hammerstein-Wiener | $y(k) = h(G(q)f(u(k))) + e(k)$ | $m + n_a + n_b + p - 2$ | Nonlinear |
| 4 | Volterra (Order $N$) | $y(k) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{\tau_1,\dots,\tau_n} h_n(\cdot) \prod_i u(k-\tau_i)$ | $M^N$ for kernel $n$ | Linear in kernels |
| 5 | Bilinear | $y(k) = \sum a_i y(k-i) + \sum b_j u(k-j) + \sum\!\sum c_{m,l} u(k-m) y(k-l) + e(k)$ | $n_a + n_b + n_c n_d$ | Linear |
| 6 | NARX (Polynomial) | $y(k) = \sum c_{i,j} u^i(k-d) y^j(k-d) + e(k)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n_u \cdot n_y)$ | Linear in $c_{i,j}$ |
| 7 | NARMAX | Above $+$ noise regressors $e(k-i)$ | Larger | Linear in $c_{i,j}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Hammerstein:** Process control (pH neutralization, distillation columns with valve nonlinearity).
> - **Wiener:** Sensor calibration (thermistor with linear thermal mass and nonlinear resistance).
> - **Volterra:** Audio amplifiers (harmonic distortion), biological tissue (drug dose-response).
> - **Bilinear:** Nuclear reactor kinetics, economic input-output models.
> - **NARX/NARMAX:** General-purpose black-box identification in MATLAB `idnlarx` and `idnlhw` toolboxes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Hammerstein Model as a Linear Regression

**Goal:** Show that the Hammerstein model with polynomial nonlinearity of degree $m$ and linear block $G(q) = \dfrac{b_1 q^{-1}}{1 + a_1 q^{-1}}$ can be cast as a *linear regression* suitable for Least Squares (LS) — provided we define appropriate regressors.

### Step 1 — Write the linear block in difference-equation form
The first-order linear block satisfies:

$$
(1 + a_1 q^{-1})\,y(k) = b_1 q^{-1}\,v(k) + e(k)
$$

Expanding the shift operator:

$$
y(k) + a_1\,y(k-1) = b_1\,v(k-1) + e(k)
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the static nonlinearity
Substitute the polynomial $v(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \gamma_i\,u^i(k)$:

$$
y(k) + a_1\,y(k-1) = b_1 \sum_{i=1}^{m} \gamma_i\,u^i(k-1) + e(k)
$$

### Step 3 — Distribute the linear gain $b_1$ into each polynomial coefficient
Define new lumped parameters $\theta_i = b_1\,\gamma_i$:

$$
y(k) = -a_1\,y(k-1) + \sum_{i=1}^{m}\theta_i\,u^i(k-1) + e(k)
$$

### Step 4 — Write in linear regression form $y = \boldsymbol{\varphi}^T\boldsymbol{\theta} + e$
Define the regression vector and parameter vector:

$$
\boldsymbol{\varphi}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(k-1) \\ u(k-1) \\ u^2(k-1) \\ \vdots \\ u^m(k-1) \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \\ \vdots \\ \theta_m \end{bmatrix}
$$

Then the Hammerstein model becomes the standard linear regression:

$$
y(k) = \boldsymbol{\varphi}^T(k)\,\boldsymbol{\theta} + e(k)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The lumped parameters $\theta_i$ cannot uniquely recover $b_1$ and $\gamma_i$ separately; we can only recover them up to a scaling. This is the well-known *scaling ambiguity* of the Hammerstein model that KTU examiners frequently test.

### Step 5 — Stack $N$ measurements and apply ordinary least squares
For $N$ data samples:

$$
\mathbf{Y} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}\,\boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{E}
$$

The LS estimate is:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \left(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \boldsymbol{\Phi}\right)^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \mathbf{Y}
$$

provided the matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^T \boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is full rank (persistent excitation of order $m+1$ in $u$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Volterra Series Reduction to Hammerstein

**Goal:** Demonstrate that if the second-order Volterra kernel has a *separable* (rank-1) structure, the model reduces to a Hammerstein model.

### Step 1 — Write a second-order Volterra model
$$
y(k) = \sum_{\tau_1=0}^{M-1} h_1(\tau_1)\,u(k-\tau_1) + \sum_{\tau_1=0}^{M-1}\sum_{\tau_2=0}^{M-1} h_2(\tau_1,\tau_2)\,u(k-\tau_1)u(k-\tau_2) + e(k)
$$

### Step 2 — Assume the rank-1 separability of the second-order kernel
$$
h_2(\tau_1,\tau_2) = \alpha_1(\tau_1)\,\alpha_2(\tau_2)
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and factor
$$
y(k) = \sum_{\tau_1} h_1(\tau_1)\,u(k-\tau_1) + \Bigl[\sum_{\tau_1}\alpha_1(\tau_1)u(k-\tau_1)\Bigr]\Bigl[\sum_{\tau_2}\alpha_2(\tau_2)u(k-\tau_2)\Bigr] + e(k)
$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the quadratic-input Hammerstein structure
Let $g_1(\tau) = h_1(\tau)$ and $g_2(\tau) = \alpha_1(\tau) = \alpha_2(\tau)$. The intermediate signal $v(k) = u(k) + \bigl[g_2 \ast u\bigr](k)$ is fed through the linear filter with impulse response $g_1$. This is precisely the **degree-2 Hammerstein model**, confirming the structural equivalence.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Wiener Model Output from a Sine Input

**Goal:** Compute the output of a Wiener model with quadratic output nonlinearity when the input is a pure sinusoid $u(k) = A\sin(\omega k)$, and the linear block has gain $G(\mathrm{e}^{j\omega}) = \vert G\vert\,\mathrm{e}^{j\phi}$.

### Step 1 — Linear block output
$$
w(k) = A\,\vert G(\mathrm{e}^{j\omega})\vert\,\sin(\omega k + \phi)
$$

For brevity, let $B = A\,\vert G\vert$:

$$
w(k) = B\sin(\omega k + \phi)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply quadratic output nonlinearity $h(w) = \beta_1 w + \beta_2 w^2$
$$
y(k) = \beta_1 B\sin(\omega k + \phi) + \beta_2 B^2 \sin^2(\omega k + \phi)
$$

### Step 3 — Use the identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$

$$
y(k) = \beta_1 B\sin(\omega k + \phi) + \frac{\beta_2 B^2}{2} - \frac{\beta_2 B^2}{2}\cos\bigl(2\omega k + 2\phi\bigr)
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the result
The output contains:
- A **DC bias** of magnitude $\dfrac{\beta_2 B^2}{2}$ (rectification effect of the squaring block),
- A **fundamental** at frequency $\omega$ with amplitude $\beta_1 B$,
- A **second harmonic** at frequency $2\omega$ with amplitude $\dfrac{\beta_2 B^2}{2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single-tone test on a Wiener system produces harmonics in the output even though the input has only one frequency. The presence of harmonics is a *signature* of nonlinearity and is exploited in **nonlinearity tests** (e.g., the Wiener–Volterra expansion test, the bent-line test).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Model for a Given Plant

| Plant Characteristic | Recommended Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Input saturation only (e.g., actuator) | **Hammerstein** | NL at input, linear LTI dynamics after |
| Output compression only (e.g., sensor) | **Wiener** | Linear dynamics with NL sensor |
| Both input + output NL (typical industrial plant) | **Hammerstein-Wiener** | Captures both ends |
| Mild cross-coupling, no clear static NL | **Bilinear** | Multiplicative interaction |
| Strongly nonlinear with no separable structure | **NARX / NARMAX** | General black-box |
| High-order memory, mildly nonlinear | **Truncated Volterra (Laguerre/Kautz)** | Dimension reduction |
| Slowly drifting parameters | **Recursive identification on any of the above** | Tracks time variation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hammerstein Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT U K] --> B[STATIC NL F]
    B --> C[INTERMEDIATE V K]
    C --> D[LINEAR DYNAMICS G Q]
    D --> E[OUTPUT Y K]
    F[NOISE E K] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows left to right. $u(k)$ enters the static nonlinearity $f(\cdot)$ which produces the intermediate $v(k)$. $v(k)$ is filtered by the linear dynamic block $G(q)$ to yield $y(k)$. Measurement noise $e(k)$ corrupts the output.

## 4.2 Wiener Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT U K] --> B[LINEAR DYNAMICS G Q]
    B --> C[INTERMEDIATE W K]
    C --> D[STATIC NL H]
    D --> E[OUTPUT Y K]
    F[NOISE E K] --> E
```

## 4.3 Hammerstein-Wiener (Sandwich) Model — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT U K] --> B[STATIC NL F INPUT]
    B --> C[INTERMEDIATE V K]
    C --> D[LINEAR DYNAMICS G Q]
    D --> E[INTERMEDIATE W K]
    E --> F[STATIC NL H OUTPUT]
    F --> G[OUTPUT Y K]
    H[NOISE E K] --> G
```

## 4.4 Bilinear Model — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[INPUT U K] --> B[LINEAR INPUT PATH B Q]
    A --> C[BILINEAR CROSS PRODUCT]
    D[PAST OUTPUT Y K MINUS 1] --> E[LINEAR OUTPUT PATH A Q]
    D --> C
    C --> F[SUMMER]
    B --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[OUTPUT Y K]
    H[NOISE E K] --> G
```

## 4.5 NARX Polynomial Model — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[INPUT U K] --> B[TAP DELAY LINE NU]
    D[OUTPUT Y K] --> E[TAP DELAY LINE NY]
    B --> F[NONLINEAR POLYNOMIAL EXPANSION]
    E --> F
    F --> G[SUMMER]
    H[NOISE E K] --> G
    G --> I[OUTPUT Y K PLUS 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read NARX architecture:** Past inputs $u(k-1), \dots, u(k-n_u)$ and past outputs $y(k-1), \dots, y(k-n_y)$ are tapped into delay lines. The nonlinear function $F^{\text{NL}}$ (typically a polynomial) maps these regressors to a predicted $\hat y(k)$. The prediction error $e(k) = y(k) - \hat y(k)$ drives the parameter adaptation in the identification phase.

## 4.6 Block-Level Comparison Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG1[Hammerstein]
        H1[NL] --> H2[L DYNAMIC] --> H3[Y]
    end
    subgraph SG2[Wiener]
        W1[L DYNAMIC] --> W2[NL] --> W3[Y]
    end
    subgraph SG3[Hammerstein-Wiener]
        HW1[NL INPUT] --> HW2[L DYNAMIC] --> HW3[NL OUTPUT] --> HW4[Y]
    end
    subgraph SG4[Bilinear]
        B1[LINEAR A] --> BX[SUMMER]
        B2[LINEAR B] --> BX
        B3[CROSS C] --> BX
        BX --> B4[Y]
    end
```
