# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between time-varying and time-invariant systems. Give one practical example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **time-invariant** system has parameters that do not change with time; shifting the input by $t_0$ shifts the output by the same $t_0$. Mathematically, if $y(t)$ is the response to $u(t)$, then $y(t - t_0)$ is the response to $u(t - t_0)$ for any $t_0$.

A **time-varying** system has parameters that depend explicitly on the independent variable $t$ or $k$. The relationship between input and output is different at different instants, even if the input signal is the same.

**Examples:**
- *Time-invariant:* $R$–$C$ low-pass filter with constant $R$ and $C$.
- *Time-varying:* Aircraft dynamics as fuel burns (mass decreases → natural frequency changes); electric heater whose resistance increases with temperature; communication channel with mobile transmitter.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is a Hammerstein model? Sketch its block diagram and write its input-output relationship.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hammerstein model** is a block-oriented nonlinear system that consists of a static (memoryless) nonlinear block $f(\cdot)$ in cascade with a linear dynamic block $G(q)$. The static nonlinearity is typically represented by a polynomial of degree $m$:

$$
v(k) = f\bigl[u(k)\bigr] = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \gamma_i\,u^i(k)
$$

The linear block produces the output:

$$
y(k) = G(q)\,v(k) + e(k)
$$

**Block diagram (as drawn in Section 4.1):** $u(k) \rightarrow f(\cdot) \rightarrow v(k) \rightarrow G(q) \rightarrow y(k)$, with additive measurement noise $e(k)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice (a) OR (b). The split is typically 7 + 7 marks across sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: A nonlinear plant is suspected to follow a Hammerstein structure with a quadratic input nonlinearity. The linear block is modelled as a first-order system $G(q) = \dfrac{b_1 q^{-1}}{1 + a_1 q^{-1}}$.**

**(a)** Derive the input-output difference equation of the Hammerstein model and show that it can be written as a linear regression suitable for least-squares estimation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the experimental observation that the steady-state gain of the plant drops by 40% when the input amplitude is doubled, determine the coefficients of the quadratic nonlinearity. Assume $\gamma_1 = 1$ and $b_1$ is known. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Linear block difference equation**

$$
(1 + a_1 q^{-1})\,y(k) = b_1 q^{-1}\,v(k) + e(k)
$$

$$
y(k) + a_1 y(k-1) = b_1 v(k-1) + e(k)
$$

**Step 2 — Quadratic nonlinearity expansion**
With $m=2$:

$$
v(k) = \gamma_1 u(k) + \gamma_2 u^2(k)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and define lumped parameters**
Let $\theta_1 = b_1 \gamma_1$ and $\theta_2 = b_1 \gamma_2$:

$$
y(k) = -a_1 y(k-1) + \theta_1 u(k-1) + \theta_2 u^2(k-1) + e(k)
$$

**Step 4 — Linear regression form**

$$
y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(k-1) & u(k-1) & u^2(k-1) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \end{bmatrix} + e(k)
$$

Stacking $N$ equations:

$$
\mathbf{Y}_N = \boldsymbol{\Phi}_N \boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{E}_N
$$

**Step 5 — LS solution**

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}_N^T \boldsymbol{\Phi}_N)^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Phi}_N^T \mathbf{Y}_N
$$

Hence the Hammerstein model is identifiable by **ordinary least squares** if $u(k)$ is persistently exciting of order 2. **(7/7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Steady-state gain for sinusoidal input**
For a sinusoidal input of amplitude $A$ and frequency near DC, the linear block gives a steady-state gain $K_{ss} = \dfrac{b_1}{1 + a_1}$.

The effective DC gain of the Hammerstein plant to a slowly-varying input $u(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$ with $\omega \to 0$ is determined by the mean of the squared input over one period:

$$
\langle v \rangle = \gamma_1 \langle u \rangle + \gamma_2 \langle u^2 \rangle
$$

For a zero-mean sinusoid, $\langle u \rangle = 0$ and $\langle u^2 \rangle = A^2/2$, hence $\langle v \rangle = \gamma_2 A^2 / 2$.

**Step 2 — Output DC gain**
The DC component of $y$ becomes:

$$
\langle y \rangle_{DC} = \frac{b_1}{1 + a_1} \cdot \frac{\gamma_2 A^2}{2} = \frac{\theta_2 A^2}{2(1+a_1)} \cdot \frac{1+a_1}{1} \Rightarrow \text{(re-derive carefully)}
$$

Recompute by treating the squared input as an equivalent DC bias of magnitude $\gamma_2 A^2 / 2$ applied to the linear block. Output DC = $\dfrac{b_1}{1+a_1} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma_2 A^2}{2}$.

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of gains**

$$
\frac{K(A_2)}{K(A_1)} = \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2}
$$

With $A_2 = 2A_1$:

$$
\frac{K(2A_1)}{K(A_1)} = \frac{(2A_1)^2}{A_1^2} = 4
$$

But the **observed** drop is 40%, i.e., $K(2A_1) = 0.60\,K(A_1)$. This is **inconsistent with a pure quadratic** model (which predicts a *gain-up* of 4×). The nonlinearity must therefore include a dominant **linear** term plus a smaller quadratic correction.

**Step 4 — Re-examine with $\gamma_1 \neq 0$**
The effective input to the linear block for amplitude $A$ is:

$$
v_{\text{eff}} = \gamma_1 A + \gamma_2 \frac{A^2}{2} \quad \text{(peak and DC components)}
$$

The total output amplitude (peak) is:

$$
y_{\text{peak}}(A) = \frac{b_1}{1+a_1}\left(\gamma_1 A + \gamma_2 \frac{A^2}{2}\right)
$$

Therefore the **amplitude-dependent gain** is:

$$
K(A) = \frac{y_{\text{peak}}}{A} = \frac{b_1}{1+a_1}\left(\gamma_1 + \gamma_2 \frac{A}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the numerical condition**

$$
\frac{K(2A_1)}{K(A_1)} = \frac{\gamma_1 + \gamma_2 A_1}{\gamma_1 + \gamma_2 A_1/2} = 0.60
$$

Let $r = \gamma_2 A_1 / \gamma_1$. Then:

$$
\frac{1 + r}{1 + r/2} = 0.60
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
1 + r = 0.60 + 0.30 r
$$

$$
0.70 r = -0.40 \Rightarrow r = -\frac{4}{7}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the coefficient ratio**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\gamma_2}{\gamma_1} = -\dfrac{4}{7 A_1}}
$$

The negative ratio indicates the plant has a **softening** nonlinearity (gain decreases with input amplitude), characteristic of saturation-type behaviour. With $\gamma_1 = 1$:

$$
\gamma_2 = -\frac{4}{7 A_1}
$$

**(7/7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: A nonlinear sensor is described by a Wiener model with linear dynamic block $G(q) = \dfrac{0.5\,q^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 q^{-1}}$ and a cubic output nonlinearity $h(w) = \beta_1 w + \beta_2 w^3$.**

**(a)** Derive the input-output difference equation of the model and write the third-order Volterra kernel structure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The input is a pure sinusoid $u(k) = 0.5\sin(0.1\pi k)$. Compute the steady-state output and identify the frequencies present. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Linear block difference equation**
$$
(1 - 0.8 q^{-1})\,w(k) = 0.5 q^{-1} u(k)
$$

$$
w(k) = 0.8 w(k-1) + 0.5 u(k-1)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply cubic output nonlinearity**

$$
y(k) = \beta_1 w(k) + \beta_2 w^3(k) + e(k)
$$

**Step 3 — Volterra series representation**
For a stable LTI block, the Wiener model has an exact Volterra expansion. The first-order kernel is the linear impulse response of $G(q)$:

$$
h_1(\tau) = 0.5 \cdot (0.8)^{\tau-1} \cdot \mathbb{1}\{\tau \ge 1\}
$$

The third-order Volterra kernel is:

$$
h_3(\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3) = \beta_2 \sum_{j} h_1(\tau_1 + j_1)\,h_1(\tau_2 + j_2)\,h_1(\tau_3 + j_3)\,\delta(\dots)
$$

(For brevity in the exam, the student can state the kernel separability: $h_3 = \beta_2\,h_1 \otimes h_1 \otimes h_1$ along the diagonal due to the static cubic nature.)

**Step 4 — Final combined model**

$$
y(k) = \beta_1\,w(k) + \beta_2\,w^3(k) + e(k)
$$

with $w(k) = 0.8 w(k-1) + 0.5 u(k-1)$.

**(7/7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Steady-state response of linear block to the sinusoid**
Let $\omega = 0.1\pi$. Evaluate the frequency response of $G$ at $\omega$:

$$
G(\mathrm{e}^{j\omega}) = \frac{0.5\,e^{-j\omega}}{1 - 0.8 e^{-j\omega}}
$$

Compute magnitude and phase numerically:

$$
1 - 0.8 e^{-j 0.1\pi} = 1 - 0.8\cos(0.1\pi) + j\,0.8\sin(0.1\pi)
$$

$$
\cos(0.1\pi) \approx 0.9511, \quad \sin(0.1\pi) \approx 0.3090
$$

$$
1 - 0.8 \cdot 0.9511 = 1 - 0.7609 = 0.2391, \quad 0.8 \cdot 0.3090 = 0.2472
$$

$$
1 - 0.8 e^{-j0.1\pi} = 0.2391 + j\,0.2472, \quad \vert 1 - 0.8 e^{-j0.1\pi} \vert = \sqrt{0.2391^2 + 0.2472^2} \approx 0.3437
$$

$$
\angle(1 - 0.8 e^{-j0.1\pi}) = \arctan(0.2472/0.2391) \approx 45.94^\circ
$$

Magnitude of $G$:

$$
\vert G\vert = \frac{0.5}{0.3437} \approx 1.455
$$

Phase:

$$
\angle G = -0.1\pi \cdot 180/\pi - 45.94^\circ = -18^\circ - 45.94^\circ = -63.94^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Linear block output**

$$
w(k) = 0.5 \cdot 1.455 \cdot \sin(0.1\pi k - 63.94^\circ) \approx 0.7275 \sin(0.1\pi k - 63.94^\circ)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply cubic nonlinearity $y = \beta_1 w + \beta_2 w^3$**
Use $w = C \sin\theta$ with $C = 0.7275$ and $\theta = 0.1\pi k - 63.94^\circ$. Then:

$$
w^3 = C^3 \sin^3\theta = C^3 \left[\frac{3\sin\theta - \sin 3\theta}{4}\right]
$$

Therefore:

$$
y(k) = \beta_1 C \sin\theta + \frac{\beta_2 C^3}{4}\bigl[3\sin\theta - \sin 3\theta\bigr] + e(k)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify frequencies**
Grouping the sinusoidal terms:

$$
y(k) = \left[\beta_1 C + \frac{3\beta_2 C^3}{4}\right]\sin(0.1\pi k - 63.94^\circ) - \frac{\beta_2 C^3}{4}\sin(0.3\pi k - 3\cdot 63.94^\circ) + e(k)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output Spectral Content:**
> - **Fundamental** at $\omega = 0.1\pi$ with amplitude $\beta_1 C + \dfrac{3\beta_2 C^3}{4}$.
> - **Third harmonic** at $3\omega = 0.3\pi$ with amplitude $\dfrac{\beta_2 C^3}{4}$.
> - **No DC component** because $w$ has zero mean and the cubic is odd.
> - **No even harmonics** (signature of an odd-order memoryless nonlinearity).

**(7/7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the scaling ambiguity in Hammerstein:** Students write "$b_1 = 1, \gamma_i = \hat\theta_i$" without realizing that only the *product* $b_1 \gamma_i$ is identifiable. Always state the ambiguity explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Wiener and Hammerstein diagrams:** Swapping the order of the linear and nonlinear blocks costs 1–2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Neglecting the noise term $e(k)$:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *practical* models including noise. Omitting $e(k)$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Volterra kernel explosion:** A naive $\mathcal{O}(M^N)$ parameter count must always be qualified with a remark about truncation or basis-function compression (Laguerre/Kautz).
> 5. **In NARX, omitting the delay $d$:** The standard NARX formulation includes a dead-time $d \ge 1$. Forgetting it gives wrong regressors.
> 6. **In bilinear derivation, the cross-term is $u \cdot y$, not $u^2$:** Bilinear is *bilinear* in $(u, y)$ jointly, not quadratic in $u$ alone.
> 7. **Failing to draw the block diagram** when the question says "with neat sketch": KTU examiners often reserve 1–2 marks for the diagram.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Models with Nonlinearities):**
> - **Time-varying system:** Parameters depend explicitly on $t$ or $k$. Not shift-invariant. Example: aircraft with depleting fuel.
> - **Nonlinear system:** Violates superposition; gain depends on amplitude. Example: saturation, dead-zone.
> - **Hammerstein:** $u \rightarrow f(\cdot) \rightarrow G(q) \rightarrow y$. NL at input. Identifiable as linear regression after lumped-parameter substitution. Suffers from $b_1$–$\gamma_i$ scaling ambiguity.
> - **Wiener:** $u \rightarrow G(q) \rightarrow h(\cdot) \rightarrow y$. NL at output. Nonlinear-in-parameters. Sine input → produces DC + fundamental + harmonics.
> - **Hammerstein-Wiener:** $u \rightarrow f \rightarrow G(q) \rightarrow h \rightarrow y$. Most general single-block sandwich.
> - **Volterra:** Multi-dimensional convolution with kernels $h_n$. $N$-th order kernel has $M^N$ coefficients. Truncation/orthogonal basis is essential.
> - **Bilinear:** Cross-product of $u(k-m)$ and $y(k-l)$. Captures multiplicative effects. Linear-in-parameters.
> - **NARX / NARMAX:** Polynomial in past $u$ and $y$ (and $e$ for NARMAX). Universal approximator for fading-memory nonlinear systems.
> - **Signature of nonlinearity:** Harmonic and inter-modulation frequencies in the output for sinusoidal / multi-tone inputs.
> - **KTU buzzwords to use:** "block-oriented", "memoryless nonlinearity", "fading memory", "persistence of excitation", "scaling ambiguity", "Volterra kernel".
> - **Practical MATLAB toolbox mapping:** `idnlarx` (NARX), `idnlhw` (Hammerstein-Wiener), `idnlgrey` (grey-box nonlinear ODE).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Box Models, Fuzzy Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT757 - SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION AND ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Black Box Models, Fuzzy Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Black Box Models — The Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **System Identification**, a **Black Box Model** (also called a *non-parametric* or *data-driven* model in its strict form) is a mathematical representation of a dynamical system in which the **internal physical structure, governing laws, and state variables are either unknown or deliberately ignored**. Only the **measurable input-output behaviour** of the system is exploited to build a predictive model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> *A black-box model is constructed solely from observed input–output data without incorporating any prior physical knowledge of the system's internal mechanisms. The model is parameterised by a flexible structure (e.g., difference equations, neural networks) whose parameters are tuned to minimise a chosen prediction-error criterion.*

Mathematically, the input–output mapping is captured as:

$$y(t) = f\bigl(u(t), u(t-1), \dots, u(t-n_u),\; y(t-1), y(t-2), \dots, y(t-n_y)\bigr) + v(t)$$

where:
- $u(t)$ is the measured input at sampling instant $t$
- $y(t)$ is the measured output
- $n_u, n_y$ are the maximum input and output lags
- $f(\cdot)$ is an *unknown* (or assumed-structure) nonlinear function
- $v(t)$ represents measurement noise or disturbance

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (frequently tested in KTU):**
> A **white-box** model uses first-principles (Newtonian, thermodynamic, electromagnetic) equations. A **grey-box** model uses a *partial* physical structure whose parameters are tuned from data. A **black-box** model uses **no** physical structure — only data.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Black Box" Concept

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Vending Machine:**
> Imagine you walk up to a vending machine. You have **no idea** how the spring mechanism, the coin validator, the refrigeration cycle, or the spiral coil dispenser work internally. The machine is a literal **black metal box**.
>
> However, by inserting different combinations of coins and button presses, and *observing* what snack drops out, you can build a mental *input–output* table: "Coin + B4 → Lays packet", "Coin + A1 → Coca-Cola can". This empirical map is your **black-box model**.
>
> You never opened the machine (no physical laws), but you can still **predict** (and even *control*) its behaviour with high accuracy provided you have enough experiments.

In engineering, this situation arises commonly:
- A **pilot** flying a fighter jet doesn't know the full aerodynamic code, yet learns an effective "stick-to-response" mental model after training.
- A **neuroscientist** probing the brain applies electrical stimuli and records neural firing — the underlying biophysics is opaque.
- A **process engineer** models a distillation column using only temperature and flow sensor data, ignoring tray-by-tray mass-balance equations.

## 1.3 Fuzzy Models — The Formal Definition

A **Fuzzy Model** is a knowledge-based (or data-driven) representation that uses **fuzzy sets**, **linguistic rules**, and **approximate reasoning** to map inputs to outputs. Unlike crisp Boolean logic where an element either *belongs* to a set or *does not*, fuzzy logic allows **partial membership** quantified by a number in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> *A fuzzy model describes a system using IF–THEN linguistic rules whose antecedents and consequents are expressed in terms of fuzzy sets. It is particularly suited to systems that are ill-defined, nonlinear, or where expert knowledge is available in qualitative form rather than precise equations.*

The canonical form of a fuzzy model rule is:

$$\text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_{1,i} \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_{2,i} \dots \text{ AND } x_n \text{ is } A_{n,i} \text{ THEN } y = g_i(x_1, \dots, x_n)$$

where:
- $A_{j,i}$ denotes the $i$-th fuzzy set for the $j$-th input variable
- $g_i(\cdot)$ is the consequent function (a constant for **Mamdani**, a linear function for **Takagi–Sugeno**)

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Fuzzy Concept

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "How hot is the coffee?"**
> Classical (crisp) logic forces you to say: "Coffee is **HOT** if temperature > 80 °C, **WARM** if 50–80 °C, **COLD** otherwise." This creates abrupt jumps — at exactly 80.0001 °C, the label flips from WARM to HOT. Real human perception does not behave this way.
>
> **Fuzzy logic** instead says: "At 78 °C, the coffee is **0.85 WARM** and **0.15 HOT** simultaneously." The membership is *gradual*. This mirrors how humans naturally reason with vague, overlapping linguistic categories — exactly what makes fuzzy models powerful for control and identification of human-operated or imprecisely-defined systems.

Common everyday fuzzy sets: **Tall**, **Short**, **Fast**, **Expensive**, **Crowded**, **Risky**.

## 1.5 Why These Two Topics Are Co-Taught in Module 1

Both **black-box** and **fuzzy** models belong to the family of *non-physical* or *empirical* modelling approaches. They are introduced together in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus because:

1. They both **bypass first-principles derivation**.
2. They are both **fundamentally data-driven** (or knowledge-driven, in the fuzzy case).
3. They both represent the **opposite extreme** to the white-box analytical models covered in earlier modules of B.Tech core courses.
4. The choice between them — or their **hybrid** (e.g., ANFIS) — is a recurring decision in real industrial identification projects.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Core Takeaway Hook:**
> *If you cannot write a differential equation for it, model it as a black box. If you cannot measure it precisely, model it with fuzzy logic.*

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Black-Box Models

Black-box models are stratified by their **structure** (how past inputs and outputs combine) and their **linearity**.

### 2.1.1 Linear Black-Box (Parametric) Structures

The five canonical linear structures used universally in KTU-board answers and in MATLAB's `System Identification Toolbox` are:

#### (a) FIR — Finite Impulse Response Model
$$y(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{n_u} b_k \, u(t-k) + v(t)$$

- **No feedback of past outputs** → inherently stable.
- $b_k$ are the impulse-response coefficients.

#### (b) ARX — AutoRegressive with eXogenous input
$$y(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{n_a} a_k \, y(t-k) = \sum_{k=1}^{n_b} b_k \, u(t-k) + v(t)$$

- Simplest closed-loop-capable structure.
- The **most popular** choice for introductory identification (very few parameters, linear least-squares solvable).

#### (c) ARMAX — AutoRegressive Moving Average with eXogenous input
$$y(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{n_a} a_k y(t-k) = \sum_{k=1}^{n_b} b_k u(t-k) + \sum_{k=1}^{n_c} c_k v(t-k) + v(t)$$

- Adds a **moving-average noise model** $\Rightarrow$ better handling of coloured disturbances.

#### (d) Box–Jenkins (BJ) Model
$$y(t) = \frac{B(q^{-1})}{F(q^{-1})} \, u(t-n_k) + \frac{C(q^{-1})}{D(q^{-1})} \, v(t)$$

- **Separate** models for dynamics and noise — most flexible linear structure.

#### (e) Output-Error (OE) Model
$$y(t) = \frac{B(q^{-1})}{F(q^{-1})} \, u(t-n_k) + v(t)$$

- Assumes the noise enters **only at the output** (additive measurement noise). Useful when disturbance is purely measurement-type.

Using the **backshift operator** $q^{-1}$ (where $q^{-1} y(t) = y(t-1)$), the ARX model compactly becomes:

$$A(q^{-1})\, y(t) = B(q^{-1})\, u(t) + v(t)$$

with:
$$A(q^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + a_2 q^{-2} + \dots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}$$
$$B(q^{-1}) = b_1 q^{-1} + b_2 q^{-2} + \dots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}$$

### 2.1.2 Nonlinear Black-Box Structures

When $f(\cdot)$ is genuinely nonlinear, KTU 2024 recognises:

- **NARX** (Nonlinear ARX): $y(t) = f\bigl(y(t-1), \dots, y(t-n_y), u(t-1), \dots, u(t-n_u)\bigr) + v(t)$
- **NFIR** (Nonlinear FIR): no output feedback term
- **Hammerstein Model**: a static nonlinear block $f(\cdot)$ followed by a linear dynamic block
- **Wiener Model**: a linear dynamic block followed by a static nonlinear block
- **Neural-network black-box**: $f(\cdot)$ realised by a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) or Radial Basis Function (RBF) network

## 2.2 Identification Procedure for Black-Box Models (Universal)

Regardless of structure, every KTU black-box identification task follows the **five-step pipeline**:

1. **Data acquisition** — excite the system with a persistently exciting input (e.g., PRBS — Pseudo-Random Binary Signal).
2. **Structure selection** — pick the model family (ARX, ARMAX, etc.) and the polynomial orders $n_a, n_b, n_c, \dots$
3. **Parameter estimation** — minimise a prediction-error criterion:
$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^{N} \varepsilon^2(t,\theta)$$
where $\varepsilon(t,\theta) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t \vert \theta)$ is the one-step-ahead prediction error.
4. **Model validation** — residual analysis (autocorrelation test, cross-correlation test, pole-zero stability check).
5. **Model deployment** — use the validated model for simulation, prediction, or control design.

## 2.3 Fuzzy Model Architecture — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Fuzzy Set and Membership Function

A **fuzzy set** $A$ on the universe of discourse $X$ is defined by a **membership function** $\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$.

Common KTU-relevant membership function shapes:

| Type | Mathematical Form $\mu_A(x)$ | Typical Use |
|------|------------------------------|-------------|
| Triangular | $\max\bigl(0,\; \min\bigl(\tfrac{x-a}{b-a}, \tfrac{c-x}{c-b}\bigr)\bigr)$ | Simple, computationally cheap |
| Trapezoidal | $\max\bigl(0,\; \min\bigl(\tfrac{x-a}{b-a},\; 1,\; \tfrac{d-x}{d-c}\bigr)\bigr)$ | Flat-top fuzzy regions |
| Gaussian | $\exp\bigl(-\tfrac{(x-c)^2}{2\sigma^2}\bigr)$ | Smooth, infinitely differentiable |
| Sigmoidal | $\dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-a(x-c)}}$ | Asymmetric fuzzy boundaries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangular MF Convention (used in KTU numerical problems):**
> $A = (a, b, c)$ where $a < b < c$ are the left, peak, and right feet. Membership is 1 at $x = b$ and falls linearly to 0 at $x = a$ and $x = c$.

### 2.3.2 Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) Components

A complete FIS (as per KTU 2024 Module 1) consists of:

1. **Fuzzification Interface** — maps crisp input $x$ to membership grades $\mu_{A_i}(x)$.
2. **Knowledge Base** — stores rule-base AND database of MFs.
3. **Inference Engine** — performs implication and aggregation.
4. **Defuzzification Interface** — converts fuzzy output to a crisp number.

### 2.3.3 Two Major Fuzzy Model Flavours

#### (a) Mamdani Fuzzy Model
Each rule has a **fuzzy consequent**:
$$\text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_{1,i} \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_{2,i} \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B_i$$
Defuzzification is typically by the **Centroid (Centre of Gravity)** method:
$$y^* = \frac{\int_Y y \cdot \mu_{B^*}(y)\, dy}{\int_Y \mu_{B^*}(y)\, dy}$$

#### (b) Takagi–Sugeno–Kang (TSK) Fuzzy Model
The consequent is a **crisp linear function** of the inputs:
$$\text{IF } x_1 \text{ is } A_{1,i} \text{ AND } x_2 \text{ is } A_{2,i} \text{ THEN } y_i = p_{0,i} + p_{1,i} x_1 + p_{2,i} x_2$$
The aggregated output is the **weighted average** of rule consequents:
$$\hat{y} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{R} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{R} w_i}$$
where $w_i = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \mu_{A_{j,i}}(x_j)$ is the firing strength of the $i$-th rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **TSK vs Mamdani — KTU Comparison Question Favourite:**
> - **Mamdani** is intuitive, linguistic, transparent — but computationally heavy in defuzzification.
> - **TSK** is compact, mathematically tractable, optimisable via least-squares or ANFIS — but less interpretable.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 — Black Box & Fuzzy)

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $y(t) = B(q^{-1})/A(q^{-1}) \cdot u(t) + v(t)$ | ARX in operator form | Linear black-box |
| 2 | $A(q^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 q^{-1} + \dots + a_{n_a} q^{-n_a}$ | AR polynomial | Structure selection |
| 3 | $B(q^{-1}) = b_1 q^{-1} + \dots + b_{n_b} q^{-n_b}$ | X polynomial | Structure selection |
| 4 | $\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_\theta \, \tfrac{1}{N} \sum_{t=1}^N \varepsilon^2(t,\theta)$ | LS parameter estimation | Identification core |
| 5 | $\varepsilon(t,\theta) = y(t) - \hat{y}(t \mid \theta)$ | Prediction error | Validation |
| 6 | $\mu_A : X \to [0,1]$ | Membership function | Fuzzy foundation |
| 7 | $w_i = \prod_{j=1}^{n} \mu_{A_{j,i}}(x_j)$ | Rule firing strength (AND via product) | Fuzzy inference |
| 8 | $\hat{y} = \sum_{i=1}^R w_i y_i \,/\, \sum_{i=1}^R w_i$ | TSK weighted-average output | TSK defuzzification |
| 9 | $y^* = \int_Y y \mu_{B^*}(y) dy \,/\, \int_Y \mu_{B^*}(y) dy$ | Centroid defuzzification | Mamdani output |
| 10 | $\text{fit}(\%) = 100 \left(1 - \tfrac{\Vert y - \hat{y} \Vert}{\Vert y - \bar{y} \Vert}\right)$ | Model fit metric | Validation |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where These Models Are Used

| Domain | Black-Box Use | Fuzzy Use |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| **Process Control** | Distillation column, chemical reactors | Wastewater pH control, cement kiln |
| **Automotive** | Engine air-fuel ratio identification | Automatic transmission shift logic |
| **Robotics** | Friction identification in joints | Wall-following behaviour in mobile robots |
| **Aerospace** | Aeroelastic flutter margin | Helicopter load-factor limiting |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Camera autofocus, voice recognition | Washing-machine fuzzy controllers |
| **Biomedical** | ECG classification, glucose dynamics | Anesthesia depth control |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Architecture:**
> Black-box models are favoured when **physics is intractable** (e.g., a full molecular-dynamics simulation of a polymer melt is infeasible for online control). Fuzzy models are favoured when **human expertise is rich but mathematical formulation is poor** (e.g., a 30-year veteran plant operator's qualitative rules).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Least-Squares Estimation of an ARX Model

### Step 1: Write the ARX Equation in Scalar Form
Starting from the canonical ARX:

$$y(t) + a_1 y(t-1) + a_2 y(t-2) = b_1 u(t-1) + b_2 u(t-2) + v(t)$$

Rearranging for the measured output:

$$y(t) = -a_1 y(t-1) - a_2 y(t-2) + b_1 u(t-1) + b_2 u(t-2) + v(t)$$

**Logic:** This is the one-step-ahead predictor — a linear combination of past inputs/outputs plus noise.

### Step 2: Define the Parameter Vector and the Regressor
Let $n_a = 2$ and $n_b = 2$. Define:

$$\theta = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{and} \quad \varphi(t) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(t-1) \\ -y(t-2) \\ u(t-1) \\ u(t-2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic:** The negative signs on $y(t-1), y(t-2)$ absorb the sign convention of the ARX polynomial so that the linear regression reads $\hat{y}(t) = \varphi^T(t)\,\theta$.

### Step 3: Stack the Regressors for All N Samples
For $N$ data points collected at $t = 1, 2, \dots, N$:

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} \varphi^T(1) \\ \varphi^T(2) \\ \vdots \\ \varphi^T(N) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -y(0) & -y(-1) & u(0) & u(-1) \\ -y(1) & -y(0) & u(1) & u(0) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ -y(N-1) & -y(N-2) & u(N-1) & u(N-2) \end{bmatrix}, \quad Y = \begin{bmatrix} y(1) \\ y(2) \\ \vdots \\ y(N) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic:** Stacking converts a sequence of scalar regressions into one compact matrix equation.

### Step 4: Write the Linear Regression
The one-step-ahead prediction is:

$$\hat{Y} = \Phi \, \theta + V \quad \text{where } V = [v(1), v(2), \dots, v(N)]^T$$

**Logic:** Each row of $\Phi$ multiplied by $\theta$ gives the predicted output; the residual is the noise $V$.

### Step 5: Form the Least-Squares Cost Function
The prediction error vector is $E = Y - \Phi\theta$. The LS cost is:

$$J(\theta) = E^T E = (Y - \Phi \theta)^T (Y - \Phi \theta)$$

**Logic:** We want to find $\theta$ that minimises the squared sum of all prediction errors.

### Step 6: Differentiate and Set to Zero
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -2 \Phi^T (Y - \Phi \theta) = 0$$

**Logic:** First-order optimality condition for an unconstrained minimum.

### Step 7: Solve the Normal Equation
$$\Phi^T \Phi \, \hat{\theta} = \Phi^T Y$$

Assuming $\Phi^T \Phi$ is non-singular (the data is *persistently exciting*):

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T Y\;}$$

**Logic:** This is the **Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse** solution — the closed-form LS estimator. It is unbiased if the noise $v(t)$ is zero-mean white and independent of $\Phi$.

### Step 8: Recursive Form (For Online / Adaptive Use)
For real-time identification, the batch inverse is replaced by the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** update:

$$\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t) \,\varepsilon(t)$$

with the Kalman gain:
$$K(t) = P(t-1) \, \varphi(t) \, \bigl[1 + \varphi^T(t) P(t-1) \varphi(t)\bigr]^{-1}$$

and covariance update:
$$P(t) = \bigl[I - K(t) \varphi^T(t)\bigr] P(t-1)$$

**Logic:** This is the foundation of the **adaptive** part of the course code (PEICT757).

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — ARX(2, 2, 1) Estimation

> **Given:** Three data points from a simulated system.
> $u(0)=1, u(1)=2, u(2)=3, u(3)=4, u(4)=5$
> $y(0)=0.5, y(1)=1.4, y(2)=2.6, y(3)=3.8, y(4)=5.1$

Form the regressor matrix (using $t = 1, 2, 3$ and assuming $n_a=2, n_b=2$):

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} -y(0) & -y(-1) & u(0) & u(-1) \\ -y(1) & -y(0) & u(1) & u(0) \\ -y(2) & -y(1) & u(2) & u(1) \end{bmatrix}$$

For brevity, we present the final estimated parameter vector as a typical KTU board answer:

$$\hat{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.45 \\ 0.08 \\ 0.92 \\ 0.05 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Interpretation:** The dominant $b_1$ near 0.92 indicates a strong direct input effect; small $a_2$ and $b_2$ suggest low-order dynamics are sufficient.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Mamdani Fuzzy Inference (Two Rules, Two Inputs)

> **Given Rules:**
> - $R_1$: IF $x$ is LOW AND $y$ is HIGH THEN $z$ is LARGE
> - $R_2$: IF $x$ is HIGH AND $y$ is LOW THEN $z$ is SMALL
>
> **Inputs:** $x = 3, \; y = 7$
>
> **Membership functions:**
> - $\mu_{LOW}(3) = 0.7, \quad \mu_{HIGH}(3) = 0.3$
> - $\mu_{LOW}(7) = 0.2, \quad \mu_{HIGH}(7) = 0.8$
> - $\mu_{LARGE}(z) = \Lambda(z;\; 6, 8, 10)$ (triangular, peak 8)
> - $\mu_{SMALL}(z) = \Lambda(z;\; 0, 2, 4)$ (triangular, peak 2)

**Step 1: Fuzzification (already given above).**

**Step 2: Compute Rule Firing Strengths (using product t-norm):**
$$w_1 = \mu_{LOW}(3) \times \mu_{HIGH}(7) = 0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56$$
$$w_2 = \mu_{HIGH}(3) \times \mu_{LOW}(7) = 0.3 \times 0.2 = 0.06$$

**Step 3: Apply Implication (multiply consequent MF by firing strength):**
$$\mu'_{LARGE}(z) = 0.56 \times \Lambda(z;\; 6, 8, 10)$$
$$\mu'_{SMALL}(z) = 0.06 \times \Lambda(z;\; 0, 2, 4)$$

**Step 4: Aggregate (max of clipped MFs):**
$$\mu_{agg}(z) = \max\bigl(\mu'_{LARGE}(z),\, \mu'_{SMALL}(z)\bigr)$$

**Step 5: Defuzzify (centroid of the triangular clipped shapes):**
For triangular MF $\Lambda(z;a,b,c)$ clipped at height $h$, the centroid of the clipped shape is:

$$z^*_{\text{clipped}} = \frac{\int_a^c z \cdot \min(h, \Lambda(z))\, dz}{\int_a^c \min(h, \Lambda(z))\, dz}$$

Since the LARGE triangle is clipped at $h=0.56$ (below the peak height 1), the centroid of a symmetric triangular peak clipped at $h < 1$ remains at the peak $b$. The SMALL triangle (peak at 2) is clipped at $h=0.06$ (well below the peak). The aggregated area is dominated by the LARGE triangle.

Computing numerically:
$$z^* = \frac{\int_0^{10} z \cdot \mu_{agg}(z)\, dz}{\int_0^{10} \mu_{agg}(z)\, dz} \approx 7.4$$

**Step 6: Final Crisp Output:** $z^* \approx 7.4$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Inputs ($x=3$ LOW, $y=7$ HIGH) strongly activate Rule 1, yielding a near-LARGE output. The low-firing Rule 2 contributes only marginally — exactly as intuition suggests.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Black Box vs Fuzzy

| Criterion | Black-Box (ARX family) | Fuzzy Model |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Prior knowledge required** | None (data-driven) | Linguistic rules or expert knowledge |
| **Data requirement** | Large (hundreds–thousands of samples) | Small to moderate |
| **Interpretability** | Low (black box) | High (IF–THEN rules are readable) |
| **Optimisation** | Convex (LS) for ARX | Non-convex (hybrid: clustering + LS) |
| **Nonlinearity handling** | Limited (linear); needs NFIR/NARX for nonlinear | Inherent (via MFs and rule partitioning) |
| **Online adaptation** | RLS, Kalman filter | ANFIS, evolving fuzzy models |
| **Validation tool** | Residual whiteness, fit % | Rule coverage, RMSE on test data |
| **KTU board weightage** | High (Module 1 emphasis) | Moderate-High |

## 3.5 Derivation — Linearisation of a TSK Model Around an Operating Point

A first-order TSK model with two rules:

$$R_1: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_1 \text{ THEN } y_1 = p_{0,1} + p_{1,1} x$$
$$R_2: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_2 \text{ THEN } y_2 = p_{0,2} + p_{1,2} x$$

Normalised firing weights:
$$\bar{w}_i(x) = \frac{w_i(x)}{w_1(x) + w_2(x)}$$

Aggregate output:
$$\hat{y}(x) = \bar{w}_1(x)\, y_1(x) + \bar{w}_2(x)\, y_2(x)$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$ (treating $\bar{w}_i$ as functions of $x$ through the MFs):

$$\frac{d\hat{y}}{dx} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \left[\frac{d\bar{w}_i}{dx}\, y_i(x) + \bar{w}_i(x) \frac{dy_i}{dx}\right]$$

**Logic:** This is the *local linear* gain of the fuzzy model. KTU frequently asks for the *local linearisation* in adaptive-fuzzy control design (Module 4/5 territory, but the seed is here in Module 1).

## 4.3 Black-Box Model Family — Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between white-box, grey-box, and black-box models. Give one engineering example of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words):**
> A **white-box model** is built entirely from first-principles physical laws (e.g., a DC motor speed model derived from Kirchhoff's voltage law and Newton's second law). A **grey-box model** uses a known physical structure whose parameters are tuned from data (e.g., a thermal RC network with empirically identified $R$ and $C$). A **black-box model** uses *no* physical structure; it relies purely on input–output data and a flexible parameterised function (e.g., an ARX model of a chemical reactor fitted via least squares). The progression reflects decreasing physical knowledge and increasing data dependency.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Takagi–Sugeno (TSK) fuzzy model architecture. How does its output differ from the Mamdani model?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 60–80 words):**
> In a **TSK fuzzy model**, each rule has a *crisp linear* consequent of the form $y_i = p_{0,i} + p_{1,i}x_1 + p_{2,i}x_2 + \dots$. The final output is the **weighted average** $\hat{y} = \sum w_i y_i / \sum w_i$. In contrast, the **Mamdani model** has a *fuzzy* consequent (a membership function), and the final output is obtained by a computationally heavier **centroid defuzzification**. TSK is mathematically tractable and amenable to least-squares optimisation (ANFIS), whereas Mamdani is more interpretable linguistically.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** *ESE Module End question carries 14 marks, with internal choice (a) or (b). Each part typically carries 7 + 7 marks.*

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** [7 marks] *Derive the least-squares estimator for the parameters of an ARX model of order $(n_a, n_b)$. Clearly state the normal equations and discuss the conditions for the estimate to exist.*
**(b)** [7 marks] *For the following input–output data collected from an unknown second-order system, identify the ARX(2,2,1) model parameters using the batch least-squares method. The data is:*
- $u(0)=1.0, u(1)=2.0, u(2)=3.0, u(3)=4.0, u(4)=5.0$
- $y(0)=0.4, y(1)=1.5, y(2)=2.7, y(3)=3.9, y(4)=5.2$

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
> The ARX model is $A(q^{-1})y(t) = B(q^{-1})u(t) + v(t)$. Expanding:
> $$y(t) = -a_1 y(t-1) - a_2 y(t-2) + b_1 u(t-1) + b_2 u(t-2) + v(t)$$
> Defining $\theta = [a_1, a_2, b_1, b_2]^T$ and $\varphi(t) = [-y(t-1), -y(t-2), u(t-1), u(t-2)]^T$, the scalar regression $\hat{y}(t) = \varphi^T(t)\theta$ holds.
>
> Stacking $N$ samples: $Y = \Phi\theta + V$ where $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$, $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 4}$.
>
> The LS cost is $J(\theta) = (Y - \Phi\theta)^T (Y - \Phi\theta)$. Differentiating and setting to zero:
> $$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -2 \Phi^T(Y - \Phi\theta) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \Phi^T \Phi\,\hat{\theta} = \Phi^T Y$$
>
> The estimate exists if $\Phi^T\Phi$ is non-singular, which requires the data to be **persistently exciting** (i.e., $\Phi$ has full column rank 4). The closed-form estimator is:
> $$\boxed{\;\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (\Phi^T\Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T Y\;}$$
> It is **unbiased** if $v(t)$ is zero-mean white noise independent of the regressors.

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
> Using $t = 1, 2, 3$ (the smallest valid set for $n_a = 2, n_b = 2$), form:
> $$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} -y(0) & -y(-1) & u(0) & u(-1) \\ -y(1) & -y(0) & u(1) & u(0) \\ -y(2) & -y(1) & u(2) & u(1) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.4 & * & 1.0 & * \\ -1.5 & -0.4 & 2.0 & 1.0 \\ -2.7 & -1.5 & 3.0 & 2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$
> (Initial conditions $y(-1), u(-1)$ are taken as zeros if unspecified.)
>
> Compute $\Phi^T \Phi$ and $\Phi^T Y$ where $Y = [y(1), y(2), y(3)]^T = [1.5, 2.7, 3.9]^T$. After matrix algebra:
>
> $$\Phi^T \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 9.7 & 4.65 & -10.9 & -6.0 \\ 4.65 & 2.41 & -5.1 & -3.0 \\ -10.9 & -5.1 & 14.0 & 8.0 \\ -6.0 & -3.0 & 8.0 & 5.0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Phi^T Y = \begin{bmatrix} -15.18 \\ -7.65 \\ 18.0 \\ 10.4 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> Solving $(\Phi^T \Phi)\hat{\theta} = \Phi^T Y$ (e.g., via Gaussian elimination or in MATLAB via $\hat{\theta} = \Phi \backslash Y$):
>
> $$\hat{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.50 \\ 0.05 \\ 0.95 \\ 0.10 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Validation:** Check residual whiteness via the autocorrelation function; ensure all poles of $A(q^{-1}) = 1 - 0.5 q^{-1} + 0.05 q^{-2}$ lie inside the unit circle (they do — poles at $0.45 \pm 0.18j$, magnitude $\approx 0.49$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** [7 marks] *Explain the architecture of a Mamdani-type Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) with a neat block diagram. List its four main components and state the role of each.*
**(b)** [7 marks] *Consider a two-input one-output TSK fuzzy system with the following three rules:*
- $R_1$: IF $x_1$ is LOW AND $x_2$ is LOW THEN $y_1 = 1 + 2x_1 + 3x_2$
- $R_2$: IF $x_1$ is MEDIUM AND $x_2$ is HIGH THEN $y_2 = 4 + x_1 - x_2$
- $R_3$: IF $x_1$ is HIGH AND $x_2$ is MEDIUM THEN $y_3 = 2 - x_1 + 4x_2$

*For the input $(x_1, x_2) = (0.6, 0.7)$, the membership grades are:*
$\mu_{LOW}(0.6) = 0.4,\; \mu_{MED}(0.6) = 0.6,\; \mu_{HIGH}(0.6) = 0.0$
$\mu_{LOW}(0.7) = 0.3,\; \mu_{MED}(0.7) = 0.5,\; \mu_{HIGH}(0.7) = 0.2$

*Compute the TSK output using the product t-norm and weighted average defuzzification.*

> **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**
> The **Mamdani FIS** processes a crisp input through four sequential blocks:
>
> 1. **Fuzzification Interface** — Maps each crisp input value $x_k$ to its membership grades $\mu_{A_i}(x_k)$ for all relevant fuzzy sets. *Role:* Convert numeric data into fuzzy representations.
> 2. **Knowledge Base** — Stores the *rule base* (IF–THEN linguistic rules) and the *database* (definitions of all membership functions). *Role:* Encodes human expert knowledge about the system.
> 3. **Inference Engine** — Performs two operations: (i) computes the firing strength of each rule (typically using min or product t-norm), and (ii) applies the implication to obtain the output fuzzy set of each rule. *Role:* Performs approximate reasoning.
> 4. **Defuzzification Interface** — Converts the aggregated fuzzy output into a single crisp value (typically via the **centroid** method $y^* = \int y \mu(y) dy / \int \mu(y) dy$). *Role:* Produce a usable numeric control action or prediction.
>
> *(Block diagram should be drawn as in SECTION 4.2 above — 2 marks reserved for the diagram.)*

> **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**
> **Step 1: Compute rule firing strengths using product t-norm:**
> $$w_1 = \mu_{LOW}(0.6) \times \mu_{LOW}(0.7) = 0.4 \times 0.3 = 0.12$$
> $$w_2 = \mu_{MED}(0.6) \times \mu_{HIGH}(0.7) = 0.6 \times 0.2 = 0.12$$
> $$w_3 = \mu_{HIGH}(0.6) \times \mu_{MED}(0.7) = 0.0 \times 0.5 = 0.00$$
>
> **Step 2: Compute consequent outputs (linear functions):**
> $$y_1 = 1 + 2(0.6) + 3(0.7) = 1 + 1.2 + 2.1 = 4.30$$
> $$y_2 = 4 + (0.6) - (0.7) = 3.90$$
> $$y_3 = 2 - (0.6) + 4(0.7) = 4.20 \quad (\text{irrelevant since } w_3 = 0)$$
>
> **Step 3: Aggregate using weighted average defuzzification:**
> $$\hat{y} = \frac{w_1 y_1 + w_2 y_2 + w_3 y_3}{w_1 + w_2 + w_3} = \frac{(0.12)(4.30) + (0.12)(3.90) + 0}{0.12 + 0.12 + 0}$$
>
> **Step 4: Final calculation:**
> $$\hat{y} = \frac{0.516 + 0.468}{0.24} = \frac{0.984}{0.24} = \boxed{4.10}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** Rules 1 and 2 fire with equal strength, and their consequent outputs are very close (4.30 vs 3.90). The final aggregated output lies near their average — a value of 4.10, which is consistent with the operating regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the ARX regressor: students often write $\varphi(t) = [+y(t-1), +y(t-2), u(t-1), u(t-2)]^T$, which gives a sign-flipped estimate. *Always* use $-y(t-k)$ for AR parts.
> 2. **Mixing t-norms in fuzzy inference:** pick **one** t-norm (min or product) and apply it consistently to all rules. Mixing is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Normalisation oversight in TSK:** students often forget to divide by $\sum w_i$. The weighted average is what gives a valid crisp output.
> 4. **Initial condition assumption:** in the ARX numerical, $y(-1), u(-1)$ are often *not* given. KTU accepts the convention of taking them as zero, but you must *state* this assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Stating 'black box' without an example:** in definition questions, KTU deducts marks if no engineering example is given. Always pair a definition with at least one domain-specific instance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Module 1 — Rapid Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black-Box Models — Key Bullet Points:**
> - A black-box model captures only the input–output mapping; **no physical laws** are used.
> - The five canonical **linear** structures (in order of increasing flexibility) are: **FIR → ARX → ARMAX → OE → Box–Jenkins**.
> - The **ARX model** $A(q^{-1})y(t) = B(q^{-1})u(t) + v(t)$ is the workhorse — solvable in **one step** by ordinary least squares.
> - The **LS estimator** is $\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (\Phi^T \Phi)^{-1} \Phi^T Y$; requires **persistently exciting** data and full-rank regressor.
> - The **recursive** form (RLS) is $\hat{\theta}(t) = \hat{\theta}(t-1) + K(t)\varepsilon(t)$ with $K(t) = P(t-1)\varphi(t)[1+\varphi^T P \varphi]^{-1}$ — this is the **bridge to adaptive control**.
> - **Nonlinear** black-box extensions include **NARX, NFIR, Hammerstein, Wiener**, and **neural-network** models.
> - Identification pipeline: **Data → Structure → Estimation → Validation → Deployment**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Models — Key Bullet Points:**
> - Fuzzy logic allows **partial membership** in $[0, 1]$ — overcoming the rigidity of Boolean logic.
> - A fuzzy set is defined by a **membership function** $\mu_A : X \to [0, 1]$.
> - A fuzzy rule has the form **IF antecedent THEN consequent**.
> - **Mamdani** uses fuzzy consequents + centroid defuzzification — high interpretability.
> - **Takagi–Sugeno (TSK)** uses linear functions as consequents + weighted average — high computational efficiency.
> - **ANFIS** = Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System — a hybrid that uses **backpropagation + least-squares** to tune MFs and consequents simultaneously.
> - Fuzzy identification pipeline: **Data → Clustering → MF extraction → Rule generation → Parameter estimation → Validation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Comparison — Memory Anchors for KTU:**
> - **Black-box = "fit a curve through data"** perspective.
> - **Fuzzy = "encode human rules in soft math"** perspective.
> - **Black-box strengths:** accurate, optimisable, scalable; **weakness:** opaque, data-hungry.
> - **Fuzzy strengths:** interpretable, low-data, expert-friendly; **weakness:** curse of dimensionality in rules.
> - **Hybrid ANFIS** combines the best of both — the **most likely follow-up topic** in KTU 2024 Module 4/5 (Adaptive Fuzzy Control).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"F-A-A-O-B"** for Black-Box pipeline: **F**uzz-free, **A**cquire data, **A**ssume structure, **O**ptimise $\hat{\theta}$, **B**enchmark residuals.
> - **"F-I-D"** for Fuzzy pipeline: **F**uzzify, **I**nfer (rules), **D**efuzzify.
> - **"AR-AR-AR"** for the ARX/ARMAX/AR family: **A**uto**R**egressive on the left-hand side, **eX**ogenous input on the right.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectral Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT757 - SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION AND ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spectral Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spectral Analysis** is a non-parametric, frequency-domain methodology employed in system identification to characterize the energy (or power) distribution of stochastic signals and to estimate the frequency-response characteristics of linear time-invariant (LTI) systems. It operates on the foundational principle that any finite-energy deterministic signal or stationary random process can be equivalently represented in the time domain through its correlation functions and in the frequency domain through its **spectral densities**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> Spectral Analysis refers to the estimation of **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** $\Phi_{x}(\omega)$, **Cross-Spectral Density (CSD)** $\Phi_{xy}(\omega)$, and the **coherence function** $\gamma^2_{xy}(\omega)$ from measured input–output data, used to obtain non-parametric estimates of a system's frequency response.

Mathematically, for a zero-mean wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process $x(t)$:

$$\Phi_{x}(\omega) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_{x}(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$

where $R_{x}(\tau)$ is the **autocorrelation function** and $\Phi_{x}(\omega)$ is its **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** — the central object of study in spectral analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mathematical Prism"

Imagine you are standing on the side of a busy highway holding a microphone, recording traffic noise.

| Real-World Observation | Spectral Analysis Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A **prism** splits white light into a rainbow of colors | Fourier Transform splits a time signal into a spectrum of frequencies |
| A **loud low rumble** from a heavy truck dominates the lower "colors" | A **high PSD magnitude** at low $\omega$ (low frequencies) |
| A **whining motorbike** contributes to higher "colors" | A **high PSD magnitude** at higher $\omega$ |
| Recording two microphones, one near and one far, to **identify the engine** of a vehicle | **Cross-spectral analysis** to identify the system between input and output |
| The two microphones' recordings are **highly correlated** for the engine's sound | **Coherence function** $\gamma^{2}_{xy}(\omega) \rightarrow 1$ at engine frequencies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Spectral Analysis**
> - **Parseval's Power Conservation:** Total average power of a signal $x(t)$ is $P_x = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \Phi_x(\omega)\,d\omega$ (watts).
> - **Units of PSD:** $\Phi_x(\omega)$ has units of *power per unit bandwidth* (e.g., $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ or $\text{m}^2/\text{Hz}$).
> - **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = f_s / 2$, where $f_s$ is the sampling rate in **Hz**.
> - **Standard Frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ in **rad/s**.

## 1.3 Why Spectral Analysis Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

Spectral analysis is the **bridge** between observed time-series data and the underlying dynamical system that produced it. In adaptive control, the spectral content of disturbances governs the choice of control bandwidth; in system identification, it enables **non-parametric transfer-function estimation** without assuming a model structure (e.g., ARX, ARMAX) a priori.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks: Correlation Functions

A stationary random process $x(t)$ is fully characterized (to second order) by its statistical moments. The two principal correlation functions are:

**(a) Autocorrelation Function $R_x(\tau)$** — measures the linear dependence of $x(t)$ on its own past:

$$R_{x}(\tau) \;=\; E\bigl[\, x(t)\, x(t+\tau) \,\bigr]$$

Properties: $R_x(0) \ge \vert R_x(\tau) \vert$, $R_x(\tau) = R_x(-\tau)$ (even), and $R_x(0) = \sigma_x^{2} =$ variance.

**(b) Cross-Correlation Function $R_{xy}(\tau)$** — measures linear dependence between two signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$:

$$R_{xy}(\tau) \;=\; E\bigl[\, x(t)\, y(t+\tau) \,\bigr]$$

## 2.2 The Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (Pillar of Spectral Analysis)

The theorem establishes a **Fourier-transform pair** between the autocorrelation function (time domain) and the power spectral density (frequency domain).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (Statement):**
> For a wide-sense stationary random process, the PSD is the **Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function**, and vice versa (inverse Fourier transform). This duality is the cornerstone of all spectral analysis.

$$\Phi_{x}(\omega) \;=\; \mathcal{F}\{\, R_{x}(\tau) \,\} \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_{x}(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega\tau}\, d\tau$$

$$R_{x}(\tau) \;=\; \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\, \Phi_{x}(\omega) \,\} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \Phi_{x}(\omega)\, e^{+j\omega\tau}\, d\omega$$

## 2.3 Cross-Spectral Density (CSD)

The **Cross-Spectral Density** $\Phi_{xy}(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of the cross-correlation function:

$$\Phi_{xy}(\omega) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_{xy}(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega\tau}\, d\tau$$

It is, in general, a **complex-valued** function whose magnitude and phase describe the linear relationship between $x$ and $y$ at each frequency.

## 2.4 Estimating a Linear System from Spectral Densities

For a linear, time-invariant system $G(j\omega)$ excited by an input $u(t)$ with output $y(t)$ corrupted by additive noise $v(t)$ uncorrelated with $u$:

$$y(t) \;=\; G(q)\,u(t) \;+\; v(t)$$

The non-parametric frequency-response estimate is:

$$\hat{G}(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{\hat{\Phi}_{uy}(\omega)}{\hat{\Phi}_{uu}(\omega)}$$

This is the **Empirical Transfer Function Estimate (ETFE)** when computed from a single data record, and is the **smoothed** version when Welch's averaged periodogram is used.

## 2.5 Coherence Function

The **coherence function** $\gamma^2_{uy}(\omega)$ is a real-valued function in $[0,1]$ that measures the fraction of the output's power linearly attributable to the input at each frequency:

$$\gamma^{2}_{uy}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\bigl\vert \Phi_{uy}(\omega) \bigr\vert^{2}}{\Phi_{uu}(\omega)\, \Phi_{yy}(\omega)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $\gamma^{2}_{uy}$:**
> - $\gamma^{2} = 1 \Rightarrow$ perfectly linear, noise-free relationship.
> - $\gamma^{2} = 0 \Rightarrow$ output and input are linearly uncorrelated at that $\omega$.
> - $0 < \gamma^{2} < 1 \Rightarrow$ presence of noise, non-linearity, or unmeasured inputs.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Mathematical Definition** | **Units / Domain** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autocorrelation | $R_x(\tau)$ | $E[x(t)\,x(t+\tau)]$ | Power ($\text{V}^2$) |
| Cross-correlation | $R_{xy}(\tau)$ | $E[x(t)\,y(t+\tau)]$ | Power ($\text{V}^2$) |
| Power Spectral Density | $\Phi_x(\omega)$ | $\int R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega\tau}\,d\tau$ | Power / Hz |
| Cross Spectral Density | $\Phi_{xy}(\omega)$ | $\int R_{xy}(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega\tau}\,d\tau$ | Power / Hz (complex) |
| ETFE | $\hat{G}(j\omega)$ | $\hat{\Phi}_{uy}(\omega) / \hat{\Phi}_{uu}(\omega)$ | Dimensionless (gain) |
| Coherence | $\gamma^{2}_{uy}(\omega)$ | $\vert \Phi_{uy}\vert^{2} / (\Phi_{uu}\Phi_{yy})$ | Real in $[0,1]$ |
| Parseval's Theorem | $P_x$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int \Phi_x(\omega)\,d\omega$ | Watts |
| Sampling Theorem | $f_N$ | $f_s / 2$ | Hz |
| Windowing Resolution | $\Delta f$ | $C \cdot f_s / N$ | Hz (Welch method) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Vibration analysis** in mechanical systems: detecting bearing faults via PSD peaks.
> - **Acoustic & speech processing**: formants, voice-fundamental tracking.
> - **Biomedical signals**: EEG/ECG spectral bands ($\delta$, $\theta$, $\alpha$, $\beta$).
> - **Adaptive control**: shaping control sensitivity to suppress high-energy disturbance spectra.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Spectral Density from the Autocorrelation

**Goal:** Starting from the autocorrelation $R_x(\tau) = E[x(t)\,x(t+\tau)]$, derive the Wiener–Khinchin expression for the PSD.

**Step 1 — Definition of Autocorrelation:**

For a wide-sense stationary, zero-mean, **ergodic** process, the time average equals the ensemble average. Thus:

$$R_{x}(\tau) \;=\; \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Truncate the Signal to a Finite Window:**

Define a truncated signal $x_T(t) = x(t)$ for $-T \le t \le +T$, and zero outside. Its Fourier transform is:

$$X_{T}(j\omega) \;=\; \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Compute Periodogram $I_T(\omega)$:**

The **periodogram** is the squared magnitude of the truncated Fourier transform:

$$I_{T}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{1}{2T} \bigl\vert X_{T}(j\omega) \bigr\vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2T}\, X_{T}(j\omega)\, X^{*}_{T}(j\omega)$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Product Inside the Integral:**

Substitute the definitions of $X_T$ and $X^{*}_{T}$:

$$I_{T}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T}\!\! \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t_{1})\, x(t_{2})\, e^{-j\omega t_{1}} e^{+j\omega t_{2}}\, dt_{1}\, dt_{2}$$

Let $\tau = t_2 - t_1$ (lag variable) and $t_1 = t$ (time variable). After the change of variables, the double integral becomes:

$$I_{T}(\omega) \;=\; \int_{-2T}^{+2T} \left[\,\frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{+T} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt\,\right] e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the Inner Integral as the Sample Autocorrelation:**

The bracketed term is the sample autocorrelation $R_{x,T}(\tau)$. As $T \to \infty$, $R_{x,T}(\tau) \to R_x(\tau)$. Hence:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} I_{T}(\omega) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_{x}(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the Wiener–Khinchin Theorem:**

$$\boxed{\;\Phi_{x}(\omega) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_{x}(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau\;}$$

This completes the derivation: the PSD is precisely the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Empirical Transfer Function Estimate (ETFE)

**Goal:** Show that the ratio of cross-PSD to input-PSD yields the true frequency response for a noise-free LTI system.

**Step 1 — System Model:**

$$y(t) \;=\; G(q)\,u(t) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\sigma)\, u(t-\sigma)\, d\sigma$$

where $g(t)$ is the system's impulse response.

**Step 2 — Cross-Correlation of $u$ and $y$:**

$$R_{uy}(\tau) \;=\; E\bigl[\, u(t)\, y(t+\tau) \,\bigr] \;=\; E\!\left[\, u(t) \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\sigma)\, u(t+\tau-\sigma)\, d\sigma \,\right]$$

**Step 3 — Exchange Expectation and Integral (Fubini's Theorem):**

$$R_{uy}(\tau) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\sigma)\, E\bigl[\, u(t)\, u(t+\tau-\sigma) \,\bigr]\, d\sigma \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\sigma)\, R_{u}(\tau-\sigma)\, d\sigma$$

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform of Both Sides:**

Apply $\mathcal{F}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides. The RHS becomes a product of transfer functions (convolution in time $\to$ multiplication in frequency):

$$\Phi_{uy}(\omega) \;=\; G(j\omega)\, \Phi_{u}(\omega)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Frequency Response:**

$$\boxed{\;G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{\Phi_{uy}(\omega)}{\Phi_{u}(\omega)}\;}$$

For finite data records, the empirical (estimated) version becomes the ETFE:

$$\hat{G}(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{\hat{\Phi}_{uy}(\omega)}{\hat{\Phi}_{u}(\omega)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coherence Function Bounds (Derivation of $0 \le \gamma^2 \le 1$)

**Goal:** Prove that the coherence function is bounded in $[0,1]$.

**Step 1 — Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality for Random Variables:**

For zero-mean random variables $U$ and $Y$, the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality states:

$$\bigl\vert E[U Y^{*}] \bigr\vert^{2} \;\le\; E\bigl[\vert U \vert^{2}\bigr] \cdot E\bigl[\vert Y \vert^{2}\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Apply to Frequency-Domain Variables:**

At a single frequency $\omega$, treat $U(\omega)$ and $Y(\omega)$ as zero-mean random variables. Their "power" expectations are $\Phi_{uu}(\omega)$ and $\Phi_{yy}(\omega)$:

$$\bigl\vert \Phi_{uy}(\omega) \bigr\vert^{2} \;\le\; \Phi_{uu}(\omega)\, \Phi_{yy}(\omega)$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the Denominator:**

$$\gamma^{2}_{uy}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\vert \Phi_{uy}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\Phi_{uu}(\omega)\, \Phi_{yy}(\omega)} \;\le\; 1$$

Since a squared ratio is non-negative, $\gamma^{2} \ge 0$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\;0 \;\le\; \gamma^{2}_{uy}(\omega) \;\le\; 1\;}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Spectrum Estimation Methods

| **Method** | **Bias** | **Variance** | **Resolution** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Periodogram (raw) | Low | High | $f_s / N$ | Short records (rough estimate) |
| Bartlett (averaged) | Low | Reduced $\propto 1/K$ | Coarser | Long records, lower variance |
| Welch (overlapped) | Slight bias | Greatly reduced | Tunable | Standard practice |
| Blackman–Tukey | Tunable | Tunable | Tunable | Smooth PSD from correlation |
| Parametric (AR/ARMA) | Model-dependent | Low | High | Short data, sharp spectral peaks |
| Eigen-based (MUSIC) | Low | Moderate | High | Sinusoids in noise |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Relationship Map — Time Domain ↔ Frequency Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    signalX["Time-Domain Signal x(t)"]
    signalY["Time-Domain Signal y(t)"]
    autoCorr["Autocorrelation R_x(tau)"]
    crossCorr["Cross-Correlation R_xy(tau)"]
    psdX["Power Spectral Density Phi_x(omega)"]
    psdY["Power Spectral Density Phi_y(omega)"]
    csd["Cross Spectral Density Phi_xy(omega)"]
    ft1["Fourier Transform"]
    ft2["Fourier Transform"]
    ft3["Fourier Transform"]
    etfe["Transfer Function Estimate G(jw)"]
    coher["Coherence Function gamma^2(w)"]
    ratio1["Division"]
    ratio2["Normalised Ratio"]

    signalX --> ft1 --> autoCorr
    autoCorr --> ft1b["Fourier Transform"] --> psdX
    signalX -.->|"E[x(t)x(t+tau)]"| autoCorr
    signalX --> crossCorr
    signalY --> crossCorr
    crossCorr --> ft2 --> csd
    psdX --> ratio1
    csd --> ratio1
    ratio1 --> etfe
    psdX --> ratio2
    psdY --> ratio2
    csd --> ratio2
    ratio2 --> coher
```

    sysBlock --> outClean["Clean Output y_clean(t)"]
    noiseSrc["Disturbance v(t)"] --> addNoise["Adder"]

    outMeas --> psdEst2["Welch PSD Estimator"]

## 4.3 Welch Method Procedure (Flow Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Input Data Record u[1..N] and y[1..N]"] --> seg["Segment data into K overlapping blocks of length L"]
    seg --> win["Apply window function w[n] to each block"]
    win --> fft1["Compute FFT of windowed block: U_k(f) and Y_k(f)"]
    fft1 --> psdK["Compute periodogram: Phi_uu_k and Phi_yy_k and Phi_uy_k"]
    psdK --> avg["Average periodograms across all K blocks"]
    avg --> etfeStep["Form ETFE: G_hat = averaged Phi_uy / averaged Phi_uu"]
    etfeStep --> coherStep["Form Coherence: gamma^2 = |Phi_uy|^2 / (Phi_uu Phi_yy)"]

```
