# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**State and explain the Wiener–Khinchin theorem. Why is it fundamental to spectral analysis? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Wiener–Khinchin theorem states that for a wide-sense stationary random process $x(t)$, the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** $\Phi_x(\omega)$ and the **autocorrelation function** $R_x(\tau)$ form a **Fourier transform pair**.

$$\Phi_{x}(\omega) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_{x}(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$

$$R_{x}(\tau) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \Phi_{x}(\omega)\, e^{+j\omega \tau}\, d\omega$$

**Why fundamental:** It links the **time-domain statistical description** ($R_x(\tau)$, easy to estimate from data) with the **frequency-domain energy distribution** ($\Phi_x(\omega)$, easy to interpret physically). All practical non-parametric spectral estimators (periodogram, Welch, Blackman–Tukey) rely on this duality.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the coherence function. What does a coherence value of 0.4 at a particular frequency indicate? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **coherence function** between two signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ is defined as:

$$\gamma^{2}_{xy}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\bigl\vert \Phi_{xy}(\omega) \bigr\vert^{2}}{\Phi_{xx}(\omega)\, \Phi_{yy}(\omega)} \;\in\; [0, 1]$$

**Interpretation of $\gamma^2 = 0.4$:** At that frequency, only **40% of the output power** is linearly attributable to the input. The remaining **60%** arises from:
- Measurement noise,
- Non-linear effects, or
- Unmeasured disturbance inputs.

A coherence significantly less than unity at frequencies of interest warns the engineer that the assumed linear input–output model is **incomplete** at those frequencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the cross-spectral density $\Phi_{uy}(\omega)$, the input PSD $\Phi_{uu}(\omega)$, and the plant transfer function $G(j\omega)$ for a noise-free LTI system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unity-variance white noise $u(t)$ is the input to a plant with transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{5}{s+2}$. Compute $\Phi_{uy}(\omega)$, $\Phi_{yy}(\omega)$, and the coherence $\gamma^2_{uy}(\omega)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — System Model:**

$$y(t) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\sigma)\, u(t-\sigma)\, d\sigma$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Correlation $R_{uy}(\tau)$:**

$$R_{uy}(\tau) \;=\; E\bigl[\, u(t)\, y(t+\tau) \,\bigr] \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\sigma)\, E\bigl[\, u(t)\, u(t+\tau-\sigma) \,\bigr]\, d\sigma$$

**Step 3 — Recognize Inner Expectation as $R_u(\tau-\sigma)$:**

$$R_{uy}(\tau) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} g(\sigma)\, R_{u}(\tau-\sigma)\, d\sigma$$

**Step 4 — Take Fourier Transform (Convolution Theorem):**

Convolution in time-domain $\rightarrow$ multiplication in frequency-domain:

$$\Phi_{uy}(\omega) \;=\; G(j\omega)\, \Phi_{uu}(\omega)$$

**Step 5 — Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{\;G(j\omega) \;=\; \dfrac{\Phi_{uy}(\omega)}{\Phi_{uu}(\omega)}\;}$$

This is the **Empirical Transfer Function Estimate (ETFE)** when applied to finite measured spectra.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Substitute $G(s) = 5/(s+2)$ into Frequency Response:**

$$G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{5}{j\omega + 2}$$

**Step 2 — PSD of Unity-Variance White Noise Input:**

For white noise, $R_u(\tau) = \delta(\tau)$ and $\Phi_{uu}(\omega) = 1$ (constant).

**Step 3 — Compute $\Phi_{uy}(\omega)$:**

Using $\Phi_{uy}(\omega) = G(j\omega)\,\Phi_{uu}(\omega)$:

$$\Phi_{uy}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{5}{2 + j\omega} \;=\; \frac{5(2 - j\omega)}{4 + \omega^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Phi_{yy}(\omega)$:**

For a noise-free LTI system, $\Phi_{yy}(\omega) = \vert G(j\omega) \vert^{2}\, \Phi_{uu}(\omega)$:

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{25}{4 + \omega^{2}}$$

$$\Phi_{yy}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{25}{4 + \omega^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Coherence $\gamma^{2}_{uy}(\omega)$:**

$$\gamma^{2}_{uy}(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\vert \Phi_{uy} \vert^{2}}{\Phi_{uu}\, \Phi_{yy}} \;=\; \frac{(25)/(4 + \omega^{2})}{(1)\cdot(25)/(4 + \omega^{2})} \;=\; 1$$

**Conclusion:** $\gamma^{2}_{uy}(\omega) = 1$ for all $\omega$ — confirming **perfect linear relationship** with **zero noise**, as expected in a noise-free model.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **Welch method** of PSD estimation. How does it improve upon the raw periodogram? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a measured signal $x[n]$ of length $N = 1024$ samples, sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz, segmented into $K = 8$ overlapping blocks (50% overlap) with a Hann window of length $L = 256$, compute the **frequency resolution** $\Delta f$ and the **Nyquist frequency** $f_N$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Welch Method — Procedure:**

1. **Segment** the data $x[1..N]$ into $K$ blocks of length $L$ with overlap (typically 50%).
2. **Window** each block with a tapered window $w[n]$ (Hann, Hamming, etc.) to reduce spectral leakage.
3. **FFT** each windowed block: $X_k(f) = \text{FFT}\{w[n]\,x_k[n]\}$.
4. **Compute periodogram** for each block:

$$\hat{\Phi}^{(k)}_{xx}(f) \;=\; \frac{1}{L\, U}\, \bigl\vert X_k(f) \bigr\vert^{2}$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^{2}[n]$ is the window-energy normalization.

5. **Average** the $K$ periodograms:

$$\hat{\Phi}^{W}_{xx}(f) \;=\; \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \hat{\Phi}^{(k)}_{xx}(f)$$

**Improvements over the Raw Periodogram:**

| **Aspect** | **Raw Periodogram** | **Welch Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Variance | High, $\sim \Phi^{2}_x$ | Reduced by factor $\sim 1/K$ |
| Bias | Low | Slight increase due to windowing |
| Spectral leakage | Severe (rectangular) | Suppressed (tapered window) |
| Resolution | $f_s/N$ | $C \cdot f_s/L$ (slightly coarser) |
| Robustness | Sensitive to outliers | Averaging smooths fluctuations |

**Trade-off:** Welch sacrifices a small amount of resolution for a **large reduction in variance**, making it the standard industrial choice.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $f_s = 1000$ Hz, $L = 256$ samples (block length), $K = 8$ blocks, 50% overlap, Hann window.

**Step 1 — Nyquist Frequency:**

$$f_{N} \;=\; \frac{f_{s}}{2} \;=\; \frac{1000}{2} \;=\; 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency Resolution $\Delta f$:**

$$\Delta f \;=\; \frac{f_{s}}{L} \;=\; \frac{1000}{256} \;\approx\; 3.906 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Hann Window Constant $C$:**

For a Hann window, the equivalent noise bandwidth factor $C \approx 1.5$:

$$\Delta f_{\text{effective}} \;=\; C \cdot \frac{f_s}{L} \;\approx\; 1.5 \times 3.906 \;\approx\; 5.86 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Variance Reduction Factor:**

For 50% overlap, the effective number of independent blocks is approximately:

$$K_{\text{eff}} \;\approx\; K \cdot \left(1 - \text{overlap}\right) \;\approx\; 8 \times 0.5 \;\approx\; 4 \text{ independent averages}$$

Variance of the estimate is reduced by a factor of $K_{\text{eff}} \approx 4$ compared to the raw periodogram.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $f_N$ with $f_s$:** Students often write Nyquist = sampling frequency. Remember: $f_N = f_s/2$.
> 2. **Using $N$ instead of $L$ in resolution formula:** Resolution depends on **block length** $L$, not total record length $N$, when using Welch.
> 3. **Forgetting the window-constant $C$:** Without $C$, the resolution is underestimated.
> 4. **Assuming ETFE works for noisy systems:** The formula $G = \Phi_{uy}/\Phi_{uu}$ is biased when output noise is present. Always check $\gamma^{2} < 1$ to detect noise.
> 5. **Forgetting that PSD is even:** $\Phi_x(\omega) = \Phi_x(-\omega)$ for real signals. Negative frequencies are mirror images; report single-sided PSD for real data.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Spectral Analysis**
> - **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem:** $\Phi_x(\omega) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_x(\tau)$ — the central duality.
> - **PSD units:** Power per unit bandwidth (e.g., $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$).
> - **ETFE formula:** $G(j\omega) = \Phi_{uy}(\omega) / \Phi_{uu}(\omega)$ — fundamental non-parametric identification result.
> - **Coherence bounds:** $0 \le \gamma^2_{uy}(\omega) \le 1$; derived from Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.
> - **Cross-PSD is complex:** magnitude $\Rightarrow$ gain, phase $\Rightarrow$ phase lag between signals.
> - **Parseval's Theorem:** Total signal power = $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int \Phi_x(\omega)\,d\omega$.
> - **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = f_s/2$; signals are band-limited to $[-f_N, f_N]$.
> - **Welch vs Periodogram:** Welch uses overlapping segments + windowing + averaging → **lower variance** at the cost of slight resolution loss.
> - **Bias–Variance trade-off:** Smoothing (windowing, averaging) ↑ bias, ↓ variance. Parametric models (AR/ARMA) ↑ resolution, require model order selection.
> - **Validation check:** $\gamma^2(\omega) \approx 1$ at frequencies of interest $\Rightarrow$ linear model is adequate.
> - **Spectral Factorization:** Decomposing $\Phi_v(\omega)$ into $H(z)\,H(z^{-1})$ for noise modelling in Kalman and adaptive filters.
> - **Auto-spectrum $\Phi_{xx}$, Cross-spectrum $\Phi_{xy}$:** always remember the symmetry $\Phi_{xy}(-\omega) = \Phi^{*}_{xy}(\omega)$.
> - **Common KTU mistakes:** mixing one-sided vs two-sided PSD (factor of 2); confusing autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$ with autocovariance $C_x(\tau) = R_x(\tau) - \mu_x^2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Least Squares
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT757 - SYSTEM IDENTIFICATION AND ADAPTIVE CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Least Squares

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Least Squares (LS) Method** is a fundamental **parameter estimation technique** in system identification that determines the unknown parameters of a mathematical model by **minimizing the sum of the squares of the differences** (errors) between the observed (measured) output and the model-predicted output.

In the KTU 2024 context, for a linear-in-parameters model of the form:

$$y(k) = \phi^{T}(k)\theta + e(k)$$

the Least Squares estimator $\hat{\theta}$ is defined as the parameter vector that minimizes the **quadratic loss function**:

$$J(\theta) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} e^{2}(k) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[ y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\theta \right]^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Least Squares method forms the mathematical backbone of all offline and online (recursive) parameter estimation algorithms used in adaptive control, predictive control, and ARX/ARMAX model identification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Notation (Standard KTU convention):**
> - $y(k)$ → Measured system output at instant $k$
> - $\phi(k)$ → Regression (regressor) vector containing past inputs/outputs
> - $\theta$ → True (unknown) parameter vector
> - $\hat{\theta}$ → Estimated parameter vector
> - $e(k) \rightarrow y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}$ → Equation error / residual
> - $N$ → Total number of input-output data samples
> - $n_{\theta}$ → Number of parameters to be estimated

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **fitness coach** with 50 clients, and you want to find the **best straight line** that predicts a client's weight-loss progress over weeks. You have 50 scattered data points (week, weight-loss). No single line passes through all points perfectly.

**What do you do?**

You draw many trial lines. For each line, you calculate the **vertical gap** (error) between the data point and the line. You **square** these gaps (to make them positive and emphasize large errors) and add them up. The line with the **smallest total squared gap** is the "best fit" — this is the **Least Squares line**.

In **system identification**, the same idea applies:
- The **data points** → your recorded input-output measurements $y(k)$
- The **line** → your mathematical model $\phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}$
- The **gap** → estimation error $e(k) = y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}$
- The **best fit** → the parameter set $\hat{\theta}$ that makes the model output as close as possible to the actual output.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "squares" and not absolute values?**
> 1. Squaring makes the cost function **differentiable everywhere** → easy calculus optimization.
> 2. Large errors get **penalized more heavily** (a 10-unit error contributes 100, a 5-unit error contributes only 25).
> 3. Under **Gaussian white noise** assumptions, the LS estimator is also the **Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE)** — the most statistically efficient estimator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The LS method is used in virtually every modern engineering field — from **GPS receivers** (orbital parameter estimation), **finance** (regression-based forecasting), **biomedical signal processing** (ECG/EEG model fitting), to **flight control systems** (aerodynamic coefficient identification).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear Regression Model Setup

Consider a **discrete-time SISO system** described by a linear difference equation. Using the concept of the **regression vector**, we express the output as:

$$y(k) = \phi^{T}(k)\theta + e(k)$$

where for an **ARX model of order $n$**:

$$\phi^{T}(k) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(k-1) & -y(k-2) & \cdots & -y(k-n) & u(k-1) & u(k-2) & \cdots & u(k-n) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\theta^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{1} & a_{2} & \cdots & a_{n} & b_{1} & b_{2} & \cdots & b_{n} \end{bmatrix}$$

The model in compact form for $N$ samples:

$$Y = \Phi\theta + E$$

where:

$$Y = \begin{bmatrix} y(1) \\ y(2) \\ \vdots \\ y(N) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} \phi^{T}(1) \\ \phi^{T}(2) \\ \vdots \\ \phi^{T}(N) \end{bmatrix}, \quad E = \begin{bmatrix} e(1) \\ e(2) \\ \vdots \\ e(N) \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 2.2 The Optimization Problem

**Goal:** Find $\hat{\theta}$ such that $J(\hat{\theta}) = \min_{\theta} J(\theta)$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Form the scalar cost function by squaring and summing all residuals.
2. Since $J(\theta)$ is a **quadratic function** in $\theta$, it has a **unique global minimum** (no local minima traps).
3. Take the gradient $\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ and set it to zero.
4. Solve the resulting **normal equations** for $\hat{\theta}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** The LS solution $\hat{\theta}$ projects the output vector $Y$ onto the column space of the regression matrix $\Phi$. The residual $E = Y - \Phi\hat{\theta}$ is therefore **orthogonal** to every column of $\Phi$, which gives the condition $\Phi^{T}E = 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Linear model | $y(k) = \phi^{T}(k)\theta + e(k)$ | Compact linear-in-parameters form |
| 2 | Cost function | $J(\theta) = E^{T}E = \sum e^{2}(k)$ | Sum of squared errors |
| 3 | Vector form | $J(\theta) = (Y - \Phi\theta)^{T}(Y - \Phi\theta)$ | Matrix expression of SSE |
| 4 | Gradient | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -2\Phi^{T}Y + 2\Phi^{T}\Phi\theta$ | First derivative w.r.t. $\theta$ |
| 5 | Normal equation | $\Phi^{T}\Phi\hat{\theta} = \Phi^{T}Y$ | Optimality condition |
| 6 | **LS Estimate** | $\hat{\theta} = (\Phi^{T}\Phi)^{-1}\Phi^{T}Y$ | **Closed-form LS solution** |
| 7 | Pseudo-inverse | $\Phi^{\dagger} = (\Phi^{T}\Phi)^{-1}\Phi^{T}$ | Left pseudo-inverse of $\Phi$ |
| 8 | Weighted LS | $J_{W} = (Y - \Phi\theta)^{T}W(Y - \Phi\theta)$ | $W$ is a positive-definite weight matrix |
| 9 | Weighted estimate | $\hat{\theta}_{W} = (\Phi^{T}W\Phi)^{-1}\Phi^{T}WY$ | General weighted LS formula |
| 10 | Recursive gain | $K(k) = \dfrac{P(k-1)\phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^{T}(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)}$ | RLS correction gain vector |
| 11 | **RLS update** | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)[y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)]$ | **Recursive LS parameter update** |
| 12 | Covariance update | $P(k) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left[I - K(k)\phi^{T}(k)\right]P(k-1)$ | Inverse-Hessian (covariance) recursion |
| 13 | Forgetting factor range | $0 < \lambda \leq 1$ | $\lambda = 1$ → growing memory; $\lambda < 1$ → exponentially weighted |
| 14 | Initial covariance | $P(0) = \alpha I$, with $\alpha \to \infty$ (large) | Ensures unbiased initial estimates |
| 15 | RLS prediction error | $\varepsilon(k) = y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)$ | A priori (one-step-ahead) error |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Dimensions (KTU expectation):** $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$, $\Phi \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times n_{\theta}}$, $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\theta} \times 1}$, $W \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}$ (positive-definite), $P(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_{\theta} \times n_{\theta}}$ (symmetric positive-definite covariance).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Least Squares |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Aerodynamic coefficient identification from flight data |
| **Process Control** | ARX/ARMAX model fitting for chemical plant tuning |
| **Biomedical** | Modeling drug dose-response curves |
| **Communications** | Channel equalization in digital receivers |
| **Robotics** | Online parameter adaptation in manipulator dynamics |
| **Econometrics** | Forecasting models (GDP, inflation) |
| **Power Systems** | Load forecasting and transformer parameter identification |

---

## 2.5 Existence & Uniqueness Conditions

> [!WARNING]
> **When does the LS solution exist?**
> The matrix $\Phi^{T}\Phi$ must be **invertible**, which requires:
> 1. $\Phi$ must have **full column rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(\Phi) = n_{\theta}$.
> 2. $N \geq n_{\theta}$ (at least as many data samples as parameters).
> 3. The regressor vectors $\{\phi(k)\}$ must be **persistently exciting** of order $n_{\theta}$.

**Persistent Excitation (PE) Condition:** A signal $u(k)$ is PE of order $n$ if there exist constants $C_{1}, C_{2} > 0$ and integer $N$ such that:

$$C_{1}I \leq \sum_{k=k_{0}+1}^{k_{0}+N} \phi(k)\phi^{T}(k) \leq C_{2}I$$

If this holds for all $k_{0}$, the estimate $\hat{\theta}$ is **strongly consistent**, i.e., $\hat{\theta} \to \theta$ as $N \to \infty$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Off-Line (Batch) Least Squares Estimator

We start from the cost function in vector form:

$$J(\theta) = (Y - \Phi\theta)^{T}(Y - \Phi\theta)$$

**Step 1: Expand the quadratic expression.**

$$J(\theta) = (Y^{T} - \theta^{T}\Phi^{T})(Y - \Phi\theta)$$

Multiplying term by term (scalar expansion):

$$J(\theta) = Y^{T}Y - Y^{T}\Phi\theta - \theta^{T}\Phi^{T}Y + \theta^{T}\Phi^{T}\Phi\theta$$

**Step 2: Note the scalar symmetry property.**

Since $Y^{T}\Phi\theta$ is a scalar, $Y^{T}\Phi\theta = (\theta^{T}\Phi^{T}Y)^{T} = \theta^{T}\Phi^{T}Y$.

Therefore:

$$J(\theta) = Y^{T}Y - 2\theta^{T}\Phi^{T}Y + \theta^{T}\Phi^{T}\Phi\theta$$

**Step 3: Differentiate with respect to $\theta$.**

Using matrix calculus identities $\frac{\partial(a^{T}x)}{\partial x} = a$ and $\frac{\partial(x^{T}Ax)}{\partial x} = 2Ax$ (for symmetric $A$):

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = -2\Phi^{T}Y + 2\Phi^{T}\Phi\theta$$

**Step 4: Set the gradient to zero for the optimum.**

$$\left.\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right\vert_{\theta=\hat{\theta}} = 0$$

$$-2\Phi^{T}Y + 2\Phi^{T}\Phi\hat{\theta} = 0$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to form the normal equations.**

$$\Phi^{T}\Phi\hat{\theta} = \Phi^{T}Y$$

**Step 6: Pre-multiply by $(\Phi^{T}\Phi)^{-1}$ (assuming it exists).**

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{LS} = (\Phi^{T}\Phi)^{-1}\Phi^{T}Y}$$

**Step 7: Verify second-order condition for a minimum.**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} J(\theta)}{\partial \theta^{2}} = 2\Phi^{T}\Phi$$

Since $\Phi^{T}\Phi$ is **positive semi-definite** (and PD when $\Phi$ has full column rank), $J$ is convex, and the stationary point is indeed a **global minimum**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Algorithm

The RLS algorithm is obtained by applying the **matrix inversion lemma** to the batch solution, enabling online updates as each new sample $(u(k), y(k))$ arrives.

**Step 1: Define the covariance matrix at sample $k$.**

$$P(k) = \left[ \Phi^{T}(k)\Phi(k) \right]^{-1}$$

where $\Phi(k)$ stacks all regression vectors from 1 to $k$:

$$\Phi(k) = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi(k-1) \\ \phi^{T}(k) \end{bmatrix}, \quad Y(k) = \begin{bmatrix} Y(k-1) \\ y(k) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Expand the inverse relation at step $k$.**

$$P^{-1}(k) = \Phi^{T}(k)\Phi(k) = \Phi^{T}(k-1)\Phi(k-1) + \phi(k)\phi^{T}(k)$$

$$P^{-1}(k) = P^{-1}(k-1) + \phi(k)\phi^{T}(k)$$

**Step 3: Apply the Woodbury matrix inversion lemma.**

For matrices $A$, $U$, $C$, $V$ of compatible dimensions:

$$(A + UCV)^{-1} = A^{-1} - A^{-1}U(C^{-1} + VA^{-1}U)^{-1}VA^{-1}$$

Substituting $A = P^{-1}(k-1)$, $U = \phi(k)$, $C = 1$, $V = \phi^{T}(k)$:

$$P(k) = P(k-1) - P(k-1)\phi(k)\left[1 + \phi^{T}(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)\right]^{-1}\phi^{T}(k)P(k-1)$$

**Step 4: Define the gain vector $K(k)$.**

$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1)\phi(k)}{1 + \phi^{T}(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)}$$

This is a vector of size $n_{\theta} \times 1$ (scalar denominator).

**Step 5: Substitute $K(k)$ back into the covariance update.**

$$P(k) = P(k-1) - K(k)\phi^{T}(k)P(k-1)$$

Factoring out $P(k-1)$:

$$\boxed{P(k) = \left[I - K(k)\phi^{T}(k)\right]P(k-1)}$$

**Step 6: Derive the parameter update equation.**

The batch LS estimate at time $k$ is:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = P(k)\left[\sum_{j=1}^{k} \phi(j)y(j)\right] = P(k)\left[\Phi^{T}(k-1)Y(k-1) + \phi(k)y(k)\right]$$

Using $P(k) = P(k-1) - K(k)\phi^{T}(k)P(k-1)$ and noting that $\hat{\theta}(k-1) = P(k-1)\Phi^{T}(k-1)Y(k-1)$:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) - K(k)\phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1) + P(k)\phi(k)y(k)$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\left[y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)\right]$$

**Step 7: Recognize the a priori prediction error.**

$$\varepsilon(k) = y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)$$

Final compact RLS update:

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,\varepsilon(k)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Exponentially Weighted RLS (with Forgetting Factor)

To track **time-varying parameters**, a forgetting factor $\lambda$ is introduced:

$$J(\theta, k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \left[ y(j) - \phi^{T}(j)\theta \right]^{2}$$

With $0 < \lambda \leq 1$, recent samples receive higher weight $\lambda^{0} = 1$, while old samples decay as $\lambda^{k-j}$.

**The modified RLS equations become:**

$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1)\phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^{T}(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)}$$

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\left[y(k) - \phi^{T}(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)\right]$$

$$\boxed{P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[I - K(k)\phi^{T}(k)\right]P(k-1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The factor $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ in the covariance update **amplifies** $P(k)$ over time, ensuring the algorithm never stops adapting — it always retains a "memory window" of effective length $L_{eff} = \frac{1}{1-\lambda}$.
> - $\lambda = 1$ → $L_{eff} = \infty$ (infinite memory, no tracking of variations).
> - $\lambda = 0.95$ → $L_{eff} = 20$ samples.
> - $\lambda = 0.90$ → $L_{eff} = 10$ samples.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Scalar LS for a First-Order System

**Problem:** A first-order system $y(k) = a\,y(k-1) + b\,u(k-1) + e(k)$ is observed for $N = 3$ samples:

| $k$ | $y(k)$ | $y(k-1)$ | $u(k-1)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.1 | 0.5 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 3.4 | 2.1 | 1.2 |
| 3 | 4.0 | 3.4 | 1.5 |

Estimate $\theta = [a,\; b]^{T}$ using batch LS.

**Step 1: Build the regression matrix and output vector.**

$$\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} y(0) & u(0) \\ y(1) & u(1) \\ y(2) & u(2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1.0 \\ 2.1 & 1.2 \\ 3.4 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Y = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1 \\ 3.4 \\ 4.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\Phi^{T}\Phi$.**

$$\Phi^{T}\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 2.1 & 3.4 \\ 1.0 & 1.2 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 1.0 \\ 2.1 & 1.2 \\ 3.4 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.25 + 4.41 + 11.56 & 0.5 + 2.52 + 5.10 \\ 0.5 + 2.52 + 5.10 & 1.0 + 1.44 + 2.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Phi^{T}\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} 16.22 & 8.12 \\ 8.12 & 4.69 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\Phi^{T}Y$.**

$$\Phi^{T}Y = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5(2.1) + 2.1(3.4) + 3.4(4.0) \\ 1.0(2.1) + 1.2(3.4) + 1.5(4.0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.05 + 7.14 + 13.60 \\ 2.10 + 4.08 + 6.00 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 21.79 \\ 12.18 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute the inverse $(\Phi^{T}\Phi)^{-1}$.**

$$\det(\Phi^{T}\Phi) = (16.22)(4.69) - (8.12)^{2} = 76.07 - 65.93 = 10.14$$

$$(\Phi^{T}\Phi)^{-1} = \frac{1}{10.14}\begin{bmatrix} 4.69 & -8.12 \\ -8.12 & 16.22 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4625 & -0.8008 \\ -0.8008 & 1.5996 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Compute $\hat{\theta}$.**

$$\hat{\theta} = (\Phi^{T}\Phi)^{-1}\Phi^{T}Y = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4625 & -0.8008 \\ -0.8008 & 1.5996 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 21.79 \\ 12.18 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\hat{a} = (0.4625)(21.79) + (-0.8008)(12.18) = 10.078 - 9.754 = 0.324$$

$$\hat{b} = (-0.8008)(21.79) + (1.5996)(12.18) = -17.450 + 19.483 = 2.033$$

**Final Estimate:** $\hat{\theta} = [0.324,\; 2.033]^{T}$.

**Step 6: Verify by computing residuals.**

$$\hat{y}(1) = 0.324(0.5) + 2.033(1.0) = 2.195 \;\Rightarrow\; e(1) = 2.1 - 2.195 = -0.095$$
$$\hat{y}(2) = 0.324(2.1) + 2.033(1.2) = 3.120 \;\Rightarrow\; e(2) = 3.4 - 3.120 = 0.280$$
$$\hat{y}(3) = 0.324(3.4) + 2.033(1.5) = 4.151 \;\Rightarrow\; e(3) = 4.0 - 4.151 = -0.151$$

Total SSE: $J = 0.009 + 0.078 + 0.023 = 0.110$. $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Least Squares Identification Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the LS estimator interacts with the actual (unknown) plant in a closed-loop identification setup.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputSig["Excitation Input u(k)"] --> plantBlock["TRUE PLANT G(theta)"]
    plantBlock --> actualOut["Measured Output y(k)"]
    noiseSrc["Process Noise e(k)"] --> plantBlock
    inputSig --> regressor["Regressor Builder phi(k)"]
    actualOut --> regressor
    regressor --> lseBlock["LS ESTIMATOR Core Algorithm"]
    inputSig --> lseBlock
    lseBlock --> paramEst["Estimated Parameters theta-hat(k)"]
    paramEst --> modelBlock["ADAPTIVE MODEL G-hat"]
    regressor --> modelBlock
    modelBlock --> predOut["Predicted Output y-hat(k)"]
    predOut --> errorComp["Error Computer e(k) = y - y-hat"]
    actualOut --> errorComp
    errorComp --> lseBlock
    paramEst --> ctrlOut["Estimated Model for Adaptive Controller"]
```

---

## 4.2 Sequential Flowchart: Recursive Least Squares (RLS) Algorithm

This Mermaid flowchart captures the **recursive update cycle** of the RLS algorithm — exactly what KTU examiners expect students to draw in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA(["START: Initialize"]) --> initStep["Set theta-hat(0) = 0, P(0) = alpha I"]
    initStep --> readInput["Read new sample: y(k), u(k)"]
    readInput --> buildRegr["Form regressor vector phi(k)"]
    buildRegr --> calcErr["Compute a priori error eps(k) = y(k) - phiT(k) theta-hat(k-1)"]
    calcErr --> calcGain["Compute gain vector K(k) = P(k-1) phi(k) / lambda + phiT(k) P(k-1) phi(k)"]
    calcGain --> updateParam["Update parameters: theta-hat(k) = theta-hat(k-1) + K(k) eps(k)"]
    updateParam --> updateCov["Update covariance: P(k) = I - K(k) phiT(k) P(k-1) / lambda"]
    updateCov --> checkStop{"k equals N ?"}
    checkStop -->|No| readInput
    checkStop -->|Yes| deliverOut["Deliver Final Estimate theta-hat(N)"]
    deliverOut --> endB(["END"])
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram: Off-Line vs. On-Line (RLS) Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OFFLINE["OFF-LINE BATCH LS"]
        direction TB
        d1["Collect all N samples"] --> d2["Build Phi and Y"]
        d2 --> d3["Compute theta-hat = PhiT Phi inverse PhiT Y"]
        d3 --> d4["One-shot estimate"]
    end

    subgraph ONLINE["ON-LINE RLS"]
        direction TB
        e1["Acquire sample k"] --> e2["Update gain K(k)"]
        e2 --> e3["Update theta-hat(k)"]
        e3 --> e4["Update P(k)"]
        e4 --> e5["Real-time estimate at step k"]
    end

    OFFLINE -->|"Disadvantages: high memory, no real-time tracking"| tradeoff["Trade-off"]
    ONLINE -->|"Advantages: low memory, tracks time-variation"| tradeoff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the RLS algorithm with a block diagram," always show **all three signals** ($y(k)$, $u(k)$, $\phi(k)$) entering the estimator, and **two outputs** ($\hat{\theta}(k)$ and $P(k)$) leaving it. Include the forgetting factor $\lambda$ in your diagram — examiners award marks for it.
