# 3. Exhaustive Derivations & Analytical Walk-Throughs

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The MIT Rule for a First-Order Plant with Adjustable Gain

**Problem Setup.** Consider a first-order plant with unknown static gain $k_p$:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{k_p}{\tau s + 1}$$

The reference model (designer-specified, stable) is:

$$G_m(s) = \frac{k_m}{\tau s + 1}$$

A controller is constructed as a **single adjustable gain** $k_c$ in series with the plant, producing the closed-loop transfer function:

$$T(s) = \frac{k_c \, k_p}{(\tau s + 1) + k_c \, k_p \, k_1}$$

where $k_1$ represents a fixed inner-loop gain. The reference model is parameterized to be matched when $k_c = k_c^*$, the *ideal* controller gain.

**Step 1 — Express the output error.** The reference-model and plant outputs respond to the same reference $r(t)$:

$$y_m(s) = G_m(s) \, r(s)$$

$$y_p(s) = T(s; k_c) \, r(s)$$

The generalized error is therefore:

$$e(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define the cost function.** The MIT Rule minimizes the instantaneous squared error:

$$J(k_c) = \frac{1}{2} e^2(t)$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient with respect to $k_c$.** Using the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial k_c} = e(t) \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial k_c} = e(t) \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial k_c}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sensitivity derivative.** Since $y_p = T(s; k_c) \, r$ and $T$ is differentiable in $k_c$:

$$\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial k_c} = \frac{\partial T(s; k_c)}{\partial k_c} \, r(s) \triangleq \zeta(s) \, r(s)$$

For a plant linear in the parameter, $\zeta(s) = \partial T / \partial k_c$ is a known stable transfer function. In the simplest first-order case, $\zeta(s) = \frac{k_p}{(\tau s + 1)^2}$.

**Step 5 — Apply steepest descent (gradient flow).** The parameter is updated along the negative gradient:

$$\frac{d k_c}{dt} = -\gamma \, \frac{\partial J}{\partial k_c} = -\gamma \, e(t) \, \zeta(t)$$

Written more compactly, the **MIT Rule** is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d k_c}{dt} = -\gamma \, e(t) \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial k_c}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** The product $e \cdot (\partial y_p / \partial k_c)$ acts as a *signed correction*: if increasing $k_c$ would have increased $y_p$ (positive $\partial y_p / \partial k_c$) and $y_p$ is too high ($e > 0$), then $k_c$ must be **decreased** — exactly what the negative sign dictates. The gain $\gamma$ controls convergence speed: small $\gamma$ gives slow, robust adaptation; large $\gamma$ gives fast but potentially unstable adaptation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lyapunov-Based MRAS — Full Stability Proof

This derivation is the **gold standard** of KTU 14-mark questions on Module 4. The goal is to derive an adaptation law that is **provably stable**, not merely intuitive.

**Problem Setup.** Plant and reference model:

$$\dot{x}_p = A \, x_p + B \, u_p$$

$$\dot{x}_m = A \, x_m + B \, r$$

with $A$ stable (matching condition), $B$ known. The control law is:

$$u_p = \theta^T x_p + k_0 \, r$$

where $\theta$ is the adjustable gain vector and $k_0$ is a fixed feedforward gain. Define the state error $\epsilon = x_p - x_m$ and parameter error $\phi = \theta - \theta^*$ (with $\theta^*$ the ideal value).

**Step 1 — Derive the error dynamics.** Subtracting the two state equations:

$$\dot{\epsilon} = \dot{x}_p - \dot{x}_m = A \epsilon + B (\theta^T x_p - r \, (\theta^{*T} x_m + r - r)) \cdot \ldots$$

Re-expressing the control mismatch in terms of the parameter error $\phi = \theta - \theta^*$ and using the matching identity $A x_m + B \theta^{*T} x_m + B r = A x_m + B r$ (so that the model is internally consistent), we obtain the canonical form:

$$\dot{\epsilon} = A \epsilon + B \phi^T x_p$$

**Step 2 — Choose a Lyapunov candidate.** A natural choice combines state error and parameter error:

$$V(\epsilon, \phi) = \epsilon^T P \epsilon + \text{tr}\!\left(\phi^T \Gamma^{-1} \phi\right)$$

where $P = P^T > 0$ satisfies the **Lyapunov equation** $A^T P + P A = -Q$ for some $Q = Q^T > 0$, and $\Gamma = \Gamma^T > 0$ is a positive definite weighting matrix.

**Step 3 — Differentiate $V$ along trajectories.**

$$\dot{V} = \dot{\epsilon}^T P \epsilon + \epsilon^T P \dot{\epsilon} + 2 \, \text{tr}\!\left(\phi^T \Gamma^{-1} \dot{\phi}\right)$$

Substituting $\dot{\epsilon} = A \epsilon + B \phi^T x_p$:

$$\dot{V} = \epsilon^T (A^T P + P A) \epsilon + 2 \, \epsilon^T P B \phi^T x_p + 2 \, \text{tr}\!\left(\phi^T \Gamma^{-1} \dot{\phi}\right)$$

$$\dot{V} = -\epsilon^T Q \epsilon + 2 \, \epsilon^T P B \phi^T x_p + 2 \, \text{tr}\!\left(\phi^T \Gamma^{-1} \dot{\phi}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Use the trace-to-vector identity.** Note that $\epsilon^T P B \phi^T x_p$ is a scalar, hence equal to its trace. Moreover, $\text{tr}(\phi^T x_p \, \epsilon^T P B) = x_p^T \phi \, \epsilon^T P B$ is also a scalar. By the cyclic property of the trace:

$$\epsilon^T P B \phi^T x_p = \text{tr}\!\left( \phi^T x_p \, \epsilon^T P B \right) = \text{tr}\!\left( x_p \epsilon^T P B \, \phi^T \right)$$

Combining with the parameter-error term:

$$\dot{V} = -\epsilon^T Q \epsilon + 2 \, \text{tr}\!\left[ \phi^T \left( x_p \epsilon^T P B + \Gamma^{-1} \dot{\phi} \right) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Force $\dot{V} \le 0$ via the adaptation law.** To eliminate the second term, we choose $\dot{\phi}$ such that the bracket vanishes:

$$x_p \epsilon^T P B + \Gamma^{-1} \dot{\phi} = 0$$

Solving for $\dot{\phi} = \dot{\theta}$ (since $\dot{\phi} = \dot{\theta}$):

$$\boxed{\;\dot{\theta} = -\Gamma \, B^T P \epsilon \, x_p\;}$$

With this choice, $\dot{V} = -\epsilon^T Q \epsilon \le 0$, and since $V$ is positive definite with $\dot{V}$ negative semi-definite, **Lyapunov's direct method** guarantees that $\epsilon$ remains bounded. Furthermore, $\ddot{V}$ is bounded, so by **Barbalat's lemma**, $\dot{V} \to 0$ and therefore $\epsilon \to 0$.

**Step 6 — Express the law in measurable signals.** The matrix $B^T P$ can be absorbed into a redefined gain. Defining $\bar{\Gamma} = \Gamma \, B^T P$ and recognizing that $\epsilon = x_p - x_m$, the law becomes:

$$\dot{\theta} = -\bar{\Gamma} \, (x_p - x_m) \, x_p = -\bar{\Gamma} \, \epsilon \, x_p$$

This is a measurable **error-driven, regressor-driven** update — exactly the form expected in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Normalized MIT Rule (Stability Improvement)

The classical MIT Rule is **fragile** when the reference signal $r$ is large. A standard remedy is to **normalize** the adaptation law by the square of the reference signal magnitude.

Starting from the unnormalized law:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial \theta}$$

define the modified cost $J = \tfrac{e^2}{2(1 + \alpha \, \zeta^T \zeta)}$ where $\alpha > 0$ and $\zeta = \partial y_p / \partial \theta$. Taking the gradient:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = \frac{e}{1 + \alpha \zeta^T \zeta} \left( \frac{\partial e}{\partial \theta} - \frac{e \, \alpha \, \zeta^T (\partial \zeta / \partial \theta)}{1 + \alpha \zeta^T \zeta} \right)$$

Neglecting the second-order term (valid when $\alpha \zeta^T \zeta \gg 1$ or $\zeta^T \zeta$ varies slowly), the **normalized MIT Rule** simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\frac{\gamma \, e \, \zeta}{1 + \alpha \, \zeta^T \zeta}\;}$$

The denominator $1 + \alpha \zeta^T \zeta$ provides a **soft saturation** that prevents parameter runaway when $r$ or $\zeta$ is large — at the cost of slower adaptation when signals are small.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hyperstability / Popov Approach (Landau)

The hyperstability approach reformulates MRAS as a **nonlinear feedback system**:

- **Feedforward block (linear, time-invariant):** $G(s)$ which must be **strictly positive real (SPR)**.
- **Feedback block (nonlinear, time-varying):** produces $w(t) = \phi^T(t) \zeta(t)$, where $\phi$ is the parameter error and $\zeta$ is the regressor.

The **Popov integral inequality** that the feedback block must satisfy for hyperstability is:

$$\int_0^{t_1} w(t) \, v(t) \, dt \ge -\gamma_0^2 \quad \forall \, t_1 \ge 0$$

where $v(t)$ is the input to the nonlinear block and $\gamma_0$ is a finite constant. Substituting $w = \phi^T \zeta$ and $v$ as the relevant measurable error, this inequality is satisfied by the **integral+proportional** adaptation law:

$$\phi(t) = \int_0^t K_1 \, v(\tau) \, d\tau + K_2 \, v(t) + \phi(0)$$

with $K_1, K_2$ chosen so that the **passivity condition** holds. This is the most general result: any adaptation law of the form "integral of error plus proportional error" is hyperstable, provided the linear block is SPR.

## 3.5 Stability Conditions Summarized

| Design Method | Stability Guarantee | Tuning Parameter | Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|
| MIT Rule | Heuristic (averaging analysis required) | $\gamma$ | Fragile to large $r$ |
| Normalized MIT | Improved via soft saturation | $\gamma, \alpha$ | Better, but slower |
| Lyapunov | Guaranteed by construction | $\Gamma$ | Robust if $A$ matched |
| Hyperstability | Guaranteed if SPR + Popov hold | $K_1, K_2$ | Most general framework |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Functional Architecture

## 4.1 High-Level MRAS Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **canonical MRAS signal flow** used in KTU textbook treatments:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r["r(t) Reference Input"] --> rm["G_m(s) Reference Model"]
    r --> ctrl["Adjustable Controller C(theta,t)"]
    rm --> ym["y_m(t) Model Output"]
    ctrl --> plant["G_p(s) Unknown Plant"]
    plant --> yp["y_p(t) Plant Output"]
    ym --> sub["Summing Junction"]
    yp --> sub
    sub --> e["e(t) = y_p - y_m Generalized Error"]
    e --> adapt["Adaptation Mechanism (MIT/Lyapunov/Popov)"]
    adapt --> theta["theta(t) Updated Parameters"]
    theta --> ctrl
    sens["Sensitivity Derivative dY_p/d_theta"] --> adapt
    sens -.computed from.-> plant
```

## 4.2 Detailed Adaptation Mechanism Internal Structure

The adaptation mechanism is itself a feedback loop, accepting the error $e(t)$ and the sensitivity derivative $\zeta = \partial y_p / \partial \theta$, and producing the parameter update $\dot{\theta}$:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    errInput["e(t) Generalized Error"] --> mul1["Multiplier"]
    sensInput["zeta(t) = dY_p/d_theta"] --> mul1
    mul1 --> gain["Gain -gamma Adaptation Gain"]
    gain --> int["Integrator"]
    int --> thetaOut["theta(t) Controller Parameters"]
    thetaOut --> ctrlPlant["Drives Adjustable Controller"]
    ctrlPlant --> ypOut["y_p(t) Plant Output"]
    ypOut --> errInput
    ctrlPlant --> sensInput
```

## 4.3 MRAS Decision Tree for the Designer

When approaching a new MRAS problem, the designer traverses this decision tree to select the most appropriate design method:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Define Desired Performance"] --> refMod["Construct G_m(s) from Specs"]
    refMod --> paramChoice["Are Parameters Linear in Model?"]
    paramChoice -- Yes --> mit["Use MIT Rule"]
    paramChoice -- No --> lyap["Use Lyapunov Approach"]
    mit -- Small r or bounded zeta --> mitOk["Stable by Averaging Analysis"]
    mit -- Large r or unbounded --> mitNorm["Use Normalized MIT Rule"]
    lyap --> lyapEq["Solve A_transpose P + P A = -Q"]
    lyapEq --> lyapLaw["Adaptation Law: theta_dot = -Gamma B_transpose P epsilon x_p"]
    lyapLaw --> stable["Provably Stable Closed-Loop System"]
    mitOk --> stable
    mitNorm --> stable
```

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Comparison: MRAS vs. Conventional Feedback

A common KTU question asks students to **compare MRAS with conventional feedback control**. The following Mermaid diagram highlights the structural difference:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Conventional["Conventional Feedback Control"]
        A1["r(t)"] --> A2["Fixed Controller C(s)"]
        A2 --> A3["Plant G_p(s)"]
        A3 --> A4["y(t)"]
        A4 --> A5["Feedback -y(t)"]
        A5 --> A2
    end
    subgraph MRAStructure["MRAS Adaptive Control"]
        B1["r(t)"] --> B2["Adjustable Controller C(theta)"]
        B2 --> B3["Plant G_p(s)"]
        B3 --> B4["y_p(t)"]
        B1 --> B5["Reference Model G_m(s)"]
        B5 --> B6["y_m(t)"]
        B4 --> B7["Comparator: e = y_p - y_m"]
        B6 --> B7
        B7 --> B8["Adaptation Law"]
        B8 --> B9["theta(t) Update"]
        B9 --> B2
    end
```
