# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define BIBO stability for an LTI system. State the necessary and sufficient condition for BIBO stability in terms of the impulse response.**

**Model Answer:**
BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stability is the property of a system where every bounded input signal produces a bounded output signal. Mathematically, the system is BIBO stable if for every $u(t)$ with $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M_1 < \infty$, the output $y(t)$ satisfies $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_2 < \infty$ for all $t \geq 0$.

The **necessary and sufficient condition** is that the impulse response $h(t)$ be absolutely integrable:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

Equivalently, for a transfer function $H(s)$, all poles must lie in the open left-half of the complex plane ($\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$). **[3 marks]**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**A causal LTI system has impulse response $h(t) = e^{-2t}\cos(3t)$ for $t \geq 0$. Is the system BIBO stable? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**
Yes, the system is BIBO stable. The impulse response consists of a damped sinusoid that decays exponentially. We can bound $\vert h(t) \vert \leq e^{-2t}$ since $\vert \cos(3t) \vert \leq 1$. Therefore:
$$\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert \, dt \leq \int_0^\infty e^{-2t} \, dt = \frac{1}{2} < \infty$$

Since the impulse response is absolutely integrable, the system satisfies the BIBO stability criterion. **[3 marks]**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A unity-feedback control system has the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s-1)(s+3)}$$

Using the Routh-Hurwitz criterion, determine the range of $K$ for which the closed-loop system is BIBO stable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the relationship between BIBO stability, asymptotic stability, and Lyapunov stability for an LTI system. Under what conditions are they equivalent? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Form the closed-loop characteristic equation.**

The closed-loop transfer function is
$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

The characteristic equation is $1 + G(s) = 0$, i.e.,
$$s(s-1)(s+3) + K(s+2) = 0$$

Expand $s(s-1)(s+3)$:
$$s(s-1)(s+3) = s[(s-1)(s+3)] = s[s^2 + 2s - 3] = s^3 + 2s^2 - 3s$$

Adding $K(s+2)$:
$$s^3 + 2s^2 - 3s + Ks + 2K = 0$$
$$s^3 + 2s^2 + (K-3)s + 2K = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Routh-Hurwitz criterion.**

Construct the Routh array for $D(s) = s^3 + 2s^2 + (K-3)s + 2K$:

| | | |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $K-3$ |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $2K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{2(K-3) - 2K}{2}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $2K$ | |

Simplify the $s^1$ entry:
$$\frac{2(K-3) - 2K}{2} = \frac{2K - 6 - 2K}{2} = \frac{-6}{2} = -3$$

The Routh array becomes:

| | | |
|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | $1$ | $K-3$ |
| $s^2$ | $2$ | $2K$ |
| $s^1$ | $-3$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $2K$ | |

**Step 3 — Stability conditions.**

For BIBO stability, the first column must be strictly positive: $1 > 0$, $2 > 0$, but the $s^1$ entry is $-3 < 0$ **regardless of $K$**. The $s^0$ entry $2K$ requires $K > 0$.

**Conclusion:** There is **no value of $K$** for which all first-column entries are positive. The system is **never BIBO stable** for any positive $K$. This is because the open-loop pole at $s = 1$ (in the RHP) is a fixed feature that cannot be stabilized by proportional gain alone. **A more sophisticated controller (lead/lag compensator or PID) is required.** **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Definitions.**

- **BIBO stability:** External property. Every bounded input produces a bounded output. Equivalently, $\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ for LTI systems.
- **Asymptotic (internal) stability:** Every unforced trajectory $\dot{x} = Ax$ starting from any initial state $x(0) = x_0$ returns to the origin: $\lim_{t \to \infty} x(t) = 0$.
- **Lyapunov stability:** Every unforced trajectory starting near the origin stays near the origin. Asymptotic stability is a stronger notion (Lyapunov + convergence).

**Step 2 — Relationships for LTI systems.**

For an LTI system $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx + Du$:
- The transfer function is $H(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$.
- The **poles of $H(s)$** are a subset of the **eigenvalues of $A$**.
- If and only if the system is **completely controllable and completely observable**, then poles of $H(s)$ = eigenvalues of $A$, and:
$$\text{Asymptotic stability} \iff \text{BIBO stability}$$

**Step 3 — Counter-example to show non-equivalence.**

Consider
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad D = 0$$

The system has an unstable eigenvalue at $+1$ (unobservable since it does not appear in $B$ or $C$ when paired). Computing $H(s)$:
$$H(s) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s-1 & 0 \\ 0 & s+1 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{-1}{s+1}$$

The transfer function is stable, so the system is **BIBO stable**. However, the system is **internally unstable** because of the hidden mode at $s = +1$. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** For a discrete-time LTI system with transfer function
$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 - 0.5z + 0.06}{z^3 - 1.5z^2 + 0.9z - 0.2}$$

determine whether the system is BIBO stable. If not, suggest a control strategy to make it stable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unit-step input is applied to a system with transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{5}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$. Verify BIBO stability by computing the output bound. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Find the poles.**

Solve $z^3 - 1.5z^2 + 0.9z - 0.2 = 0$. Test $z = 0.5$:
$$0.125 - 0.375 + 0.45 - 0.2 = 0 \;\checkmark$$

So $(z - 0.5)$ is a factor. Divide to get the remaining quadratic:
$$z^3 - 1.5z^2 + 0.9z - 0.2 = (z - 0.5)(z^2 - z + 0.4)$$

Solve the quadratic: $z = \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 1.6}}{2} = 0.5 \pm 0.2 i$.

**Step 2 — Check pole locations.**

- $p_1 = 0.5$: $\vert p_1 \vert = 0.5 < 1$ ✓
- $p_{2,3} = 0.5 \pm 0.2i$: $\vert p_{2,3} \vert = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.29} \approx 0.539 < 1$ ✓

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

All poles lie **inside the unit circle**. The system **is BIBO stable**. No control redesign is required; however, for robustness one could add a pole-placement controller or use state feedback with eigenvalues well inside the unit disc. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Verify pole locations.**

$D(s) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$. Poles at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$, both in LHP. So BIBO stable.

**Step 2 — Impulse response.**

$$H(s) = \frac{5}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{5}{s+1} - \frac{5}{s+2}$$
$$h(t) = 5e^{-t} - 5e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output bound for unit-step input $u(t) = 1$.**

$$\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt = \int_0^\infty \vert 5e^{-t} - 5e^{-2t} \vert dt$$

Note that $5e^{-t} > 5e^{-2t}$ for $t > 0$, so:
$$\int_0^\infty (5e^{-t} - 5e^{-2t}) dt = 5\left[1 - \tfrac{1}{2}\right] = \tfrac{5}{2}$$

**Step 4 — BIBO output bound.**

For $\vert u(t) \vert \leq M_1 = 1$:
$$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_1 \cdot \frac{5}{2} = \frac{5}{2}$$

The actual step response (via Final Value Theorem) settles at $y(\infty) = H(0) = 5/2$, confirming the bound is tight. The system is **BIBO stable** with output bound $M_2 = 5/2$. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value in the integrability test.** Writing $\int_0^\infty h(t) dt < \infty$ is wrong — it must be $\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$. Many students lose **2 marks** here.
> 2. **Treating marginally stable systems as BIBO stable.** A system with poles on the $j\omega$-axis (e.g., $1/s^2$, $\sin(t)$) is **NOT BIBO stable** even though its response remains bounded. Strict absolute integrability fails.
> 3. **Confusing BIBO stability with asymptotic stability for non-minimal systems.** Pole-zero cancellations in $H(s)$ can hide unstable modes. Always check the *state matrix* $A$ for internal stability in state-space problems.
> 4. **Mishandling discrete-time pole tests.** Discrete-time poles must satisfy $\vert p \vert < 1$, not $\text{Re}(p) < 0$. A pole at $z = -0.5 + 0.9i$ has $\text{Re} < 0$ but $\vert p \vert > 1$, and is **unstable**.
> 5. **Routh array arithmetic errors.** A sign error in the $s^1$ row can produce an incorrect stability range. Always double-check by substituting boundary values of $K$ into the characteristic polynomial.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **BIBO stability** is an *external* property: bounded input ⟹ bounded output.
- ✅ For **LTI systems**, the impulse response must be **absolutely integrable**: $\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$.
- ✅ Equivalently, **all poles of $H(s)$** must lie in the **open LHP** (continuous-time) or **inside the unit circle** (discrete-time).
- ✅ **Marginal stability ≠ BIBO stability** — simple imaginary-axis poles give bounded but not absolutely integrable responses.
- ✅ For state-space LTI systems, BIBO stability ↔ asymptotic stability **iff** the system is controllable and observable; otherwise BIBO stability is weaker.
- ✅ BIBO stability is the **first gate** in adaptive control design — the identifier and controller must be jointly BIBO stable before any convergence proof is meaningful.
- ✅ **Tools to test:** Routh-Hurwitz (continuous), Jury/Schur-Cohn (discrete), Lyapunov equation (state-space).
- ✅ **The BIBO output bound** is $M_2 \leq M_1 \cdot \|h\|_1$, where $\|h\|_1 = \int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert dt$ is the $L_1$-norm of the impulse response.
- ✅ Pole-zero cancellation can produce a **BIBO-stable yet internally unstable** system — a classic KTU pitfall.
- ✅ For **adaptive systems**, BIBO stability is established using the **Key Technical Lemma (KTL)** or **Bellman-Gronwall Lemma** in conjunction with Lyapunov redesign.
- ✅ In **discrete-time**, shift the stability boundary: from $\text{Re}(p) = 0$ to $\vert p \vert = 1$. Use the bilinear transform $z = \frac{1+w}{1-w}$ to map the unit disc to the LHP and apply Routh-Hurwitz.
- ✅ **Memory aid:** *"Pole in the wrong half (or outside the disc) — the system will fly off!"*
